side_effects: One read-only Redfish GET of the Thermal resource.

packs.name: Dell iDRAC / IPMI (ipmitool)
packs.description: Out-of-band BMC monitoring and power control over IPMI 2.0 (RMCP+/lanplus) with ipmitool — sensor data records, full sensor readings with thresholds, the System Event Log (SEL) and its summary, chassis + power state, FRU inventory, BMC firmware (mc info) and self-test, LAN config, and DCMI power draw. Plus gated mutators: chassis power control, one-time boot device, and SEL clear. Targets a Dell iDRAC or any IPMI-over-LAN baseboard controller by hostname/IP; credentials come from the runner environment. Reads are read-only.

actions.title: Set next-boot device
actions.summary: Set the device the server boots from on its NEXT boot — `ipmitool chassis bootdev <device>`. One-time by default (the BIOS reverts to its normal boot order afterward); it does not reboot the host, so pair it with a power action. Common use: "boot to PXE once" to re-image, or "bios" to drop into setup on the next restart.
actions.description: Set the device the server boots from on its NEXT boot — `ipmitool chassis bootdev <device>`. One-time by default (the BIOS reverts to its normal boot order afterward); it does not reboot the host, so pair it with a power action. Common use: "boot to PXE once" to re-image, or "bios" to drop into setup on the next restart.
side_effects: Overrides the next boot's device selection (one-time, not persistent).
Does not power-cycle the host; the override applies at the next boot.
Changes which OS/installer the next boot lands in — wrong choice can boot an unintended image.

args.name: device
args.description: Boot device for the next boot.

examples.title: Boot to PXE on the next restart

actions.title: Chassis status (power, intrusion, faults)
actions.summary: Show the BMC chassis status — `ipmitool chassis status`. Returns System Power (on/off), Power Restore Policy and Last Power Event, plus the Chassis Intrusion, Drive Fault, and Cooling/Fan Fault flags. The fastest power + health snapshot, and the action to verify the pack can reach and authenticate to a BMC.
actions.description: Show the BMC chassis status — `ipmitool chassis status`. Returns System Power (on/off), Power Restore Policy and Last Power Event, plus the Chassis Intrusion, Drive Fault, and Cooling/Fan Fault flags. The fastest power + health snapshot, and the action to verify the pack can reach and authenticate to a BMC.

actions.title: Show DCMI power reading (watts)
actions.summary: Show real-time power draw via DCMI — `ipmitool dcmi power reading`. Returns the instantaneous, minimum, maximum, and average power consumption in watts over the BMC's sampling period, plus the sampling timestamp. The direct answer to "how much is this server drawing right now?" on BMCs that support DCMI.
actions.description: Show real-time power draw via DCMI — `ipmitool dcmi power reading`. Returns the instantaneous, minimum, maximum, and average power consumption in watts over the BMC's sampling period, plus the sampling timestamp. The direct answer to "how much is this server drawing right now?" on BMCs that support DCMI.

actions.title: Show FRU inventory
actions.summary: Show field-replaceable-unit inventory — `ipmitool fru print`. Returns board and product manufacturer, name, part number, and serial for the chassis and each FRU device (PSUs appear as their own devices). The Serial fields carry the Dell service tag. Inventory and asset data; no live readings.
actions.description: Show field-replaceable-unit inventory — `ipmitool fru print`. Returns board and product manufacturer, name, part number, and serial for the chassis and each FRU device (PSUs appear as their own devices). The Serial fields carry the Dell service tag. Inventory and asset data; no live readings.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI FRU inventory read.

actions.title: Show BMC LAN configuration
actions.summary: Show the BMC LAN channel configuration — `ipmitool lan print <channel>`. Returns the BMC IP source (static/DHCP), address, netmask, MAC, default gateway, VLAN id, and the per-cipher-suite privilege map. Useful for confirming BMC network settings and which cipher suites are enabled. The SNMP community string in this output is redacted before it leaves the host.
actions.description: Show the BMC LAN channel configuration — `ipmitool lan print <channel>`. Returns the BMC IP source (static/DHCP), address, netmask, MAC, default gateway, VLAN id, and the per-cipher-suite privilege map. Useful for confirming BMC network settings and which cipher suites are enabled. The SNMP community string in this output is redacted before it leaves the host.

args.name: channel
args.description: IPMI LAN channel number (Dell iDRAC IPMI LAN is channel 1).

actions.title: Show BMC info (firmware, capabilities)
actions.summary: Show management-controller identity — `ipmitool mc info`. Returns the BMC device id, firmware revision, IPMI version, manufacturer (Dell = 674) and product ids, and the list of supported capabilities (SDR repo, SEL, FRU, chassis, …). Confirms the BMC firmware level and what it can do.
actions.description: Show management-controller identity — `ipmitool mc info`. Returns the BMC device id, firmware revision, IPMI version, manufacturer (Dell = 674) and product ids, and the list of supported capabilities (SDR repo, SEL, FRU, chassis, …). Confirms the BMC firmware level and what it can do.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI management-controller info query.

actions.title: Run BMC self-test
actions.summary: Get the BMC self-test result — `ipmitool mc selftest`. A passing controller prints "Selftest: passed"; a failure reports the specific fault (SDR repo empty, FRU corrupt, …). A quick "is the management controller itself healthy?" check. Reads the controller's own diagnostics; changes nothing.
actions.description: Get the BMC self-test result — `ipmitool mc selftest`. A passing controller prints "Selftest: passed"; a failure reports the specific fault (SDR repo empty, FRU corrupt, …). A quick "is the management controller itself healthy?" check. Reads the controller's own diagnostics; changes nothing.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI controller self-test query.
Read-only — reports existing self-test state, does not reset the BMC.

actions.title: Control chassis power
actions.summary: Change the server's power state — `ipmitool chassis power <op>`. Only "soft" is graceful (it sends an ACPI shutdown the OS can act on); "off", "cycle", "reset", and "diag" are abrupt and can lose in-flight data — treat them like pulling the plug. Prefer an OS-level shutdown or the iDRAC graceful path when one is available; reach for this when the host is unresponsive. Confirm the current state with power_status first.
actions.description: Change the server's power state — `ipmitool chassis power <op>`. Only "soft" is graceful (it sends an ACPI shutdown the OS can act on); "off", "cycle", "reset", and "diag" are abrupt and can lose in-flight data — treat them like pulling the plug. Prefer an OS-level shutdown or the iDRAC graceful path when one is available; reach for this when the host is unresponsive. Confirm the current state with power_status first.
side_effects: off: immediate hard power-down (ACPI S4/S5) — NOT an OS-coordinated shutdown; unsaved data can be lost.
soft: graceful ACPI shutdown the OS performs in an orderly way (the only graceful option; the OS may delay or veto it).
reset: hard reset with no power-off.
diag: pulses an NMI to the CPUs (triggers a crash/diagnostic dump), not a power-state change.
All non-graceful options drop every running service and open connection at once.

args.name: operation
args.description: Power operation. "soft" is graceful (ACPI); "off", "cycle", "reset", and "diag" are abrupt. "on" powers up.

actions.title: Chassis power state (on/off)
actions.summary: Show whether the server is powered on or off — `ipmitool chassis power status`. Returns a single line, "Chassis Power is on" or "... off". The cheapest check before or after a power action.
actions.description: Show whether the server is powered on or off — `ipmitool chassis power status`. Returns a single line, "Chassis Power is on" or "... off". The cheapest check before or after a power action.

actions.title: List all sensors (SDR)
actions.summary: List every sensor with its current reading and state — `ipmitool sdr elist`. One row per sensor data record: name, reading, status (ok / nc / cr), plus the sensor number and entity id. Covers temperatures, fans, voltages, currents, power supplies, and discrete state sensors in a single call — the workhorse health read.
actions.description: List every sensor with its current reading and state — `ipmitool sdr elist`. One row per sensor data record: name, reading, status (ok / nc / cr), plus the sensor number and entity id. Covers temperatures, fans, voltages, currents, power supplies, and discrete state sensors in a single call — the workhorse health read.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI SDR repository read.

actions.title: List sensors of one type
actions.summary: List only the sensors of a chosen type — `ipmitool sdr type <type>`. Narrows the SDR read to Temperature, Fan, Voltage, Current, or Power Supply so you get just the readings you care about (e.g. all inlet/exhaust temps, or all PSU sensors) without scanning the full repository.
actions.description: List only the sensors of a chosen type — `ipmitool sdr type <type>`. Narrows the SDR read to Temperature, Fan, Voltage, Current, or Power Supply so you get just the readings you care about (e.g. all inlet/exhaust temps, or all PSU sensors) without scanning the full repository.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI SDR read filtered by sensor type.

actions.title: List System Event Log entries
actions.summary: List the BMC System Event Log — `ipmitool sel elist`. One row per event: record id, date/time, sensor, decoded description, and asserted/deasserted. The authoritative history of hardware faults (ECC errors, PSU loss, thermal trips, intrusion). The iDRAC SEL is bounded and circular (512-1024 entries), so a full log can be large.
actions.description: List the BMC System Event Log — `ipmitool sel elist`. One row per event: record id, date/time, sensor, decoded description, and asserted/deasserted. The authoritative history of hardware faults (ECC errors, PSU loss, thermal trips, intrusion). The iDRAC SEL is bounded and circular (512-1024 entries), so a full log can be large.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI System Event Log read.

actions.title: Clear the System Event Log
actions.summary: Erase the BMC System Event Log — `ipmitool sel clear`. IRREVERSIBLE: every recorded hardware event is wiped and cannot be recovered. Pull the log with sel first if you need a record. Routine after addressing the faults a server logged (e.g. to reset a full SEL), but destroys forensic history, so it is gated.
actions.description: Erase the BMC System Event Log — `ipmitool sel clear`. IRREVERSIBLE: every recorded hardware event is wiped and cannot be recovered. Pull the log with sel first if you need a record. Routine after addressing the faults a server logged (e.g. to reset a full SEL), but destroys forensic history, so it is gated.
side_effects: Irreversibly erases ALL System Event Log entries on the BMC.
Cannot be undone; prior hardware-fault history is lost.
Does not affect the running OS or power state.

examples.title: Clear the event log after addressing faults

actions.title: Show SEL summary (count, capacity)
actions.summary: Show the System Event Log summary — `ipmitool sel info`. Returns the entry count, free space, percent used, last-add and last-erase timestamps, and the overflow flag, without dumping every entry. Use it to decide whether the SEL is filling up or has new events before pulling the full log.
actions.description: Show the System Event Log summary — `ipmitool sel info`. Returns the entry count, free space, percent used, last-add and last-erase timestamps, and the overflow flag, without dumping every entry. Use it to decide whether the SEL is filling up or has new events before pulling the full log.
side_effects: One read-only IPMI SEL info query.

actions.title: List sensors with thresholds
actions.summary: List sensors with their full threshold set — `ipmitool sensor list`. Like the SDR read but wider: each row adds the lower/upper non-critical, critical, and non-recoverable thresholds alongside the reading and unit. Use when you need to see how close a reading is to tripping, not just its current state. Larger output than sdr.
actions.description: List sensors with their full threshold set — `ipmitool sensor list`. Like the SDR read but wider: each row adds the lower/upper non-critical, critical, and non-recoverable thresholds alongside the reading and unit. Use when you need to see how close a reading is to tripping, not just its current state. Larger output than sdr.

packs.name: RHEL/Fedora package ops
packs.description: Counterpart to the `debian` pack for RHEL/CentOS/Fedora/Alma/Rocky: rpm inventory, dnf check-update, narrow install/remove actions for a single named package. Equivalent risk model to the apt counterpart.

actions.title: dnf autoremove -y
actions.summary: Remove packages installed as dependencies that are no longer required by any installed package. Useful for /var/cache cleanup after a large rollback. Read the dry-run before approving (the list can be long).
actions.description: Remove packages installed as dependencies that are no longer required by any installed package. Useful for /var/cache cleanup after a large rollback. Read the dry-run before approving (the list can be long).
side_effects: Dependency mistakes can be ugly — review the action output.

actions.title: dnf reinstall -y <pkg>
actions.summary: Reinstall one package to fix a corrupted on-disk state (missing files, bad checksums). Same version, same config.
actions.description: Reinstall one package to fix a corrupted on-disk state (missing files, bad checksums). Same version, same config.
side_effects: Package files restored from the repo.
Service may restart if scriptlets do that.

actions.title: dnf upgrade -y <pkg>
actions.summary: Upgrade one named package to the latest available version. Use for CVE remediation. Other packages untouched.
actions.description: Upgrade one named package to the latest available version. Use for CVE remediation. Other packages untouched.
side_effects: Service backed by the package may restart (depending on the package).
System briefly inconsistent until upgrade completes.

actions.title: dnf check-update
actions.summary: List available updates. Exits 100 if updates available — non-zero IS the success signal.
actions.description: List available updates. Exits 100 if updates available — non-zero IS the success signal.
side_effects: Refreshes metadata cache from configured repos.

actions.title: dnf clean metadata
actions.summary: Wipes cached repo metadata. Next dnf call refetches.
actions.description: Wipes cached repo metadata. Next dnf call refetches.
side_effects: Next operation downloads fresh metadata (one-time slowdown).

actions.title: dnf history
actions.summary: List recent DNF transactions (installs / removes / updates).
actions.description: List recent DNF transactions (installs / removes / updates).

actions.title: dnf install -y <pkg>
actions.summary: Installs one package + its dependencies.
actions.description: Installs one package + its dependencies.
side_effects: May restart services declared by the package's scriptlets.

actions.title: dnf remove -y <pkg>
actions.summary: Removes one package + dependencies that no longer have a reverse-dep.
actions.description: Removes one package + dependencies that no longer have a reverse-dep.
side_effects: Files installed by the package are deleted.
Other packages depending on it may also be removed.

actions.title: dnf repolist
actions.summary: List enabled repositories with package counts.
actions.description: List enabled repositories with package counts.

actions.title: rpm -qa
actions.summary: List all installed RPMs.
actions.description: List all installed RPMs.

actions.title: rpm -qi <pkg>
actions.summary: Show details for one package — version, release, install date, signer.
actions.description: Show details for one package — version, release, install date, signer.

actions.title: rpm -ql <pkg>
actions.summary: List all files installed by one package.
actions.description: List all files installed by one package.

actions.title: rpm -Va (verify all)
actions.summary: Verifies every installed file's checksum / size / perms against the rpm. Lists only mismatches.
actions.description: Verifies every installed file's checksum / size / perms against the rpm. Lists only mismatches.
side_effects: Reads every package's files for verification — IO-heavy.

packs.name: Docker operations pack
packs.description: Operator pack for Docker hosts: read-only inventory and per-container introspection, plus narrow mutators (restart, stop, kill, prune). Includes docker-compose support. The runner uid must be in the docker group; this pack does NOT escalate.

actions.title: docker compose logs (last N lines)
actions.summary: Returns the last N lines of logs for one service in a compose project. Read-only.
actions.description: Returns the last N lines of logs for one service in a compose project. Read-only.

args.name: service
args.description: Service name from the compose file.

actions.title: docker compose ps -a
actions.summary: Lists services in a compose project (running + stopped). The compose-file path is required and pattern-restricted. Read-only.
actions.description: Lists services in a compose project (running + stopped). The compose-file path is required and pattern-restricted. Read-only.

examples.title: Status of services in the prod stack

actions.title: docker compose restart (one service)
actions.summary: Restarts ONE service in a compose project. SIGTERM with the configured stop_grace_period, then SIGKILL, then start. In-flight requests are dropped.
actions.description: Restarts ONE service in a compose project. SIGTERM with the configured stop_grace_period, then SIGKILL, then start. In-flight requests are dropped.
side_effects: Containers restart and re-run their entrypoint.

actions.title: docker top (processes inside a container)
actions.summary: Lists processes running inside one container (via `docker top`). Use when the container's CPU is hot but you don't know which child process is the offender. Read-only.
actions.description: Lists processes running inside one container (via `docker top`). Use when the container's CPU is hot but you don't know which child process is the offender. Read-only.

actions.title: docker events — lifecycle + failures (bounded window)
actions.summary: Replay docker daemon events from the last N minutes, focused on container lifecycle and failures (create, start, restart, stop, die, kill, oom, destroy, health_status) — the "why did the container restart / OOM / go unhealthy?" read. Routine health-check exec_* chatter is excluded by default so the real signal is not drowned out; set include_exec=true to add exec_create/exec_start/ exec_die. Read-only.
actions.description: Replay docker daemon events from the last N minutes, focused on container lifecycle and failures (create, start, restart, stop, die, kill, oom, destroy, health_status) — the "why did the container restart / OOM / go unhealthy?" read. Routine health-check exec_* chatter is excluded by default so the real signal is not drowned out; set include_exec=true to add exec_create/exec_start/ exec_die. Read-only.
side_effects: One docker events query bounded to the requested window.

args.name: minutes
args.description: How many minutes of events to replay.

args.name: include_exec
args.description: When true, also show the routine exec_create/exec_start/exec_die events (health-check chatter). Default false.

examples.title: Recent restarts / OOM / health changes (last 5 minutes)

actions.title: docker history

actions.title: docker kill (signal)
actions.summary: Sends a Unix signal to one container's main process. Default is SIGKILL — immediate, no graceful drain. Specify `signal:` for SIGTERM (graceful) or SIGHUP (reload).
actions.description: Sends a Unix signal to one container's main process. Default is SIGKILL — immediate, no graceful drain. Specify `signal:` for SIGTERM (graceful) or SIGHUP (reload).
side_effects: Sends the chosen signal directly to the container's PID 1.
SIGKILL: instant termination; in-flight requests dropped.

args.name: signal
args.description: Signal name (KILL, TERM, HUP, USR1, USR2, INT, QUIT).

actions.title: docker logs (last N lines)
actions.summary: Returns the last N log lines for one container. Container name is pattern-restricted to alnum + "_-.". Read-only. Output passes through the runner's redaction pipeline.
actions.description: Returns the last N log lines for one container. Container name is pattern-restricted to alnum + "_-.". Read-only. Output passes through the runner's redaction pipeline.

actions.title: docker pull
actions.summary: Pulls one image from its configured registry. Network + disk intensive; image-ref restricted to safe characters. Idempotent — re-pulling an existing tag re-checks the digest.
actions.description: Pulls one image from its configured registry. Network + disk intensive; image-ref restricted to safe characters. Idempotent — re-pulling an existing tag re-checks the digest.

actions.title: docker restart (one container)
actions.summary: Restarts one container. Sends SIGTERM, waits for the configured stop-timeout, then SIGKILL, then re-starts. In-flight requests are dropped; the container's restart policy still applies after this call. Container name is pattern-restricted.
actions.description: Restarts one container. Sends SIGTERM, waits for the configured stop-timeout, then SIGKILL, then re-starts. In-flight requests are dropped; the container's restart policy still applies after this call. Container name is pattern-restricted.
side_effects: SIGTERM (then SIGKILL) is sent to the container.
In-flight requests on that container are dropped.
Container restarts and re-runs its entrypoint.

args.name: timeout
args.description: Seconds to wait between SIGTERM and SIGKILL.

actions.title: docker stats (one shot)

actions.summary: Removes stopped containers, dangling images, and unused networks. Does NOT remove unused volumes (intentional — volume data is the riskiest thing to delete). Run `docker.system_df` first to scope what will be freed. Irreversible.

actions.title: POST /<index>/_flush (flush index)
actions.summary: Flushes the index — forces a Lucene commit so the translog is cleared, which speeds recovery if a shard later restarts. Plain flush; the synced-flush API (/_flush/synced, sync IDs) was deprecated in 7.6 and removed in ES 8.0 — a normal flush gives the same recovery benefit there.
actions.description: Flushes the index — forces a Lucene commit so the translog is cleared, which speeds recovery if a shard later restarts. Plain flush; the synced-flush API (/_flush/synced, sync IDs) was deprecated in 7.6 and removed in ES 8.0 — a normal flush gives the same recovery benefit there.
side_effects: Brief pause to write segments to disk.

packs.name: Elixir / BEAM runtime
packs.description: Diagnostics for Elixir and Erlang/BEAM applications on the runner host or inside Docker containers: toolchain versions, EPMD registrations, BEAM OS process state, Linux /proc memory and process-tree detail, release RPC snapshots, supervisor/process/ETS/allocator/scheduler/port introspection, binary-leak checks, and bounded recon_trace call tracing. Releases are discovered from running BEAM processes; target one by release name, or by container for a release inside Docker.

actions.title: elixir --version
actions.summary: Show the installed Elixir and Erlang/OTP versions.
actions.description: Show the installed Elixir and Erlang/OTP versions.

actions.summary: List Erlang nodes registered with the local EPMD daemon.
actions.description: List Erlang nodes registered with the local EPMD daemon.

actions.summary: Show the installed Erlang/OTP system version.
actions.description: Show the installed Erlang/OTP system version.
side_effects: Starts a short-lived local Erlang VM.

actions.summary: Show Linux resource limits for a BEAM process.
actions.description: Show Linux resource limits for a BEAM process.

actions.title: BEAM process memory summary
actions.summary: Show Linux /proc memory, fd, thread, and mapping counts for a BEAM process.
actions.description: Show Linux /proc memory, fd, thread, and mapping counts for a BEAM process.
side_effects: Reads selected /proc files for one PID.
Does not read process argv, environment, memory contents, or file descriptor targets.

actions.title: BEAM /proc status summary
actions.summary: Show selected /proc/PID/status fields for a BEAM process.
actions.description: Show selected /proc/PID/status fields for a BEAM process.

actions.title: BEAM process tree
actions.summary: Show ancestors and descendants for a BEAM OS process without command-line arguments.
actions.description: Show ancestors and descendants for a BEAM OS process without command-line arguments.
side_effects: Does not print command-line arguments or environment variables.

actions.title: BEAM process list
actions.summary: List BEAM and Elixir OS processes on the runner host.
actions.description: List BEAM and Elixir OS processes on the runner host.
side_effects: One process table read without command-line arguments.

actions.title: Bounded recon_trace call capture
actions.summary: Trace calls to one existing module/function in the running Elixir release for a bounded time window.
actions.description: Trace calls to one existing module/function in the running Elixir release for a bounded time window.
side_effects: Resolves the target release from running BEAM processes (reads /proc; docker exec when container is set).
Installs recon_trace call tracing in the running release, then clears it after duration_ms.
Can add runtime overhead while active.
Clears recon_trace state at the end of the capture window.
include_args or include_return prints raw call arguments and return values into the captured output, which is stored in the audit trail and returned to the caller.

Raw BEAM terms routinely carry secrets and PII: a Plug.Conn holds secret_key_base and session data, connection state holds DB and API credentials, and function arguments hold whatever the caller passed.

The runner's always-on redaction only catches secrets in a few text shapes and does not reliably mask BEAM term output. Leave both flags false unless the traced function is known not to touch sensitive data.

args.name: module
args.description: Existing module atom to trace, for example MyApp.Worker, Elixir.MyApp.Worker, or gen_server.

args.name: pid_scope
args.description: Which processes recon_trace should target. Keep the default `all` to catch processes spawned during the window (servers handle each request in a fresh process); `existing` restricts to processes already alive when tracing starts.

args.name: include_args
args.description: Print raw call arguments instead of arity-only call lines. Leaks whatever the callers passed (secrets, PII) into the audit trail; the runner's redaction does not reliably mask BEAM terms. Keep false unless the traced function's arguments are known safe.

args.name: include_return
args.description: Print raw return values via recon_trace return tracing. Same exposure as include_args (a returned Plug.Conn carries secret_key_base and session data). Keep false unless the return is known safe.

args.name: container
args.description: Docker container name or ID when the release runs inside a container (see beam.release_targets); omit for a release running directly on the host.

args.name: release
args.description: Release name when several Elixir releases run on the target (see beam.release_targets); omit when only one runs or ELIXIR_RELEASE_CTL pins the target.

packs.name: Envoy proxy / service mesh
packs.description: Envoy admin-API ops: cluster + listener + runtime + config + cert inventory, server info, logging level read/write, plus traffic-shifting mutators (drain listeners, healthcheck fail/ok, reset counters) for planned failovers and incident response. Default admin URL http://127.0.0.1:9901; override via ENVOY_ADMIN env.

actions.title: GET /certs
actions.summary: List active TLS certs known to Envoy — subjects, expiry, days remaining.
actions.description: List active TLS certs known to Envoy — subjects, expiry, days remaining.

actions.title: GET /clusters
actions.summary: List all clusters with endpoint health + stats. Use for "which upstream is unhealthy?".
actions.description: List all clusters with endpoint health + stats. Use for "which upstream is unhealthy?".

actions.title: GET /config_dump
actions.summary: Dump full xDS config — bootstrap, clusters, routes, listeners. This surfaces the entire effective config, which can carry secrets (TLS private keys / SDS material, upstream credentials, auth headers). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
actions.description: Dump full xDS config — bootstrap, clusters, routes, listeners. This surfaces the entire effective config, which can carry secrets (TLS private keys / SDS material, upstream credentials, auth headers). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but exposes the full xDS config (may include secrets).

actions.title: POST /drain_listeners
actions.summary: Mark all listeners as draining. Existing connections close on their own as requests complete; no new connections accepted on drained listeners. Use during a planned restart to drain traffic before flipping a load balancer.
actions.description: Mark all listeners as draining. Existing connections close on their own as requests complete; no new connections accepted on drained listeners. Use during a planned restart to drain traffic before flipping a load balancer.
side_effects: All listeners stop accepting new connections.
Existing connections drain over the configured grace.
Health checks may fail externally during drain.

argv: if [ '{{ args.inboundonly }}' = 'true' ]; then curl -sSfX POST '{{ args.admin }}/drain_listeners?inboundonly'; else curl -sSfX POST '{{ args.admin }}/drain_listeners'; fi

actions.title: POST /healthcheck/fail
actions.summary: Mark Envoy unhealthy via its admin API. External health checks see failure; load balancer pulls this instance out of rotation. Use during planned maintenance to drain traffic before a restart.
actions.description: Mark Envoy unhealthy via its admin API. External health checks see failure; load balancer pulls this instance out of rotation. Use during planned maintenance to drain traffic before a restart.
side_effects: Upstream LB removes this instance from rotation.
Existing connections continue until they close.

actions.title: POST /healthcheck/ok
actions.summary: Mark Envoy healthy again. Reverses healthcheck_fail. Load balancer starts sending traffic again on the next health check.
actions.description: Mark Envoy healthy again. Reverses healthcheck_fail. Load balancer starts sending traffic again on the next health check.
side_effects: Upstream LB begins routing traffic back.

actions.title: GET /listeners
actions.summary: List all active listeners with bind address.
actions.description: List all active listeners with bind address.

actions.title: GET /logging
actions.summary: Show current per-component log levels.
actions.description: Show current per-component log levels.

actions.title: POST /logging?level=<level>
actions.summary: Sets all components to one log level. Use "debug" for triage, then revert to "info".
actions.description: Sets all components to one log level. Use "debug" for triage, then revert to "info".
side_effects: All components switch to the new level.
Log volume can spike at debug/trace.

actions.title: GET /ready
actions.summary: Check readiness — 200 if Envoy is fully initialized; 503 otherwise.
actions.description: Check readiness — 200 if Envoy is fully initialized; 503 otherwise.

actions.title: POST /reset_counters
actions.summary: Reset all Envoy stat counters to zero. Use before isolating a specific test scenario or after diagnosing a noisy historical counter. Histograms and gauges unaffected.
actions.description: Reset all Envoy stat counters to zero. Use before isolating a specific test scenario or after diagnosing a noisy historical counter. Histograms and gauges unaffected.
side_effects: In-flight diagnostics referencing old counter values lose context.

actions.title: GET /runtime
actions.summary: Show runtime overrides (feature flags + numeric overrides).
actions.description: Show runtime overrides (feature flags + numeric overrides).

actions.title: GET /server_info
actions.summary: Show Envoy version, uptime, hot-restart state, command-line.
actions.description: Show Envoy version, uptime, hot-restart state, command-line.

actions.title: GET /stats (filter)
actions.summary: Show Envoy stats matching a regex filter. Use 'cluster|listener|server' as a sane default.
actions.description: Show Envoy stats matching a regex filter. Use 'cluster|listener|server' as a sane default.

packs.name: fail2ban
packs.description: fail2ban inventory + per-jail banned-IP listings plus operator mutators for incident response: ban an IP into a jail, unban a false positive, reload jail filters from disk.

actions.title: fail2ban-client set <jail> banip <ip>
actions.summary: Add an IP to a jail's banlist manually. Use during an active attack when log-based detection is too slow. Banned IPs hit the configured action (typically iptables DROP).
actions.description: Add an IP to a jail's banlist manually. Use during an active attack when log-based detection is too slow. Banned IPs hit the configured action (typically iptables DROP).
side_effects: IP banned via the jail's configured action.
Existing connections from that IP may be dropped immediately.
Ban duration follows the jail's bantime setting.

examples.title: Ban an IP from the sshd jail

actions.title: get <jail> banip
actions.summary: List currently banned IPs in one jail.
actions.description: List currently banned IPs in one jail.

actions.title: fail2ban-client status <jail>
actions.summary: Show one jail's filter + actions + currently banned counts.
actions.description: Show one jail's filter + actions + currently banned counts.

actions.title: tail /var/log/fail2ban.log
actions.summary: Tail the last N lines of fail2ban.log.
actions.description: Tail the last N lines of fail2ban.log.

actions.title: fail2ban-client reload [jail]
actions.summary: Reload fail2ban config. Without a jail, reloads everything. With a jail, reloads that jail only (its filter, action, and settings). Existing bans are preserved.
actions.description: Reload fail2ban config. Without a jail, reloads everything. With a jail, reloads that jail only (its filter, action, and settings). Existing bans are preserved.
side_effects: Filter + action + jail settings re-read from disk.

actions.title: fail2ban-client status
actions.summary: List all configured jails + currently-active count.
actions.description: List all configured jails + currently-active count.

actions.title: set <jail> unbanip <ip>
actions.summary: Remove one IP's ban from one jail. Counter is reset; IP may be banned again on next failure.
actions.description: Remove one IP's ban from one jail. Counter is reset; IP may be banned again on next failure.
side_effects: IP removed from the jail's banned set.
Future failures will re-ban according to the jail's policy.

actions.title: fail2ban-client version
actions.summary: Show the fail2ban daemon version.
actions.description: Show the fail2ban daemon version.

packs.name: Firewall and netfilter
packs.description: iptables, nftables, conntrack, and traffic-control inspection plus narrow operator actions for incident response (block IP, unblock IP, flush chain). Rule edits are not persisted across iptables service reload — use IaC for permanent rules.

actions.title: conntrack count
actions.summary: Show active connection-tracking entry count + per-state breakdown.
actions.description: Show active connection-tracking entry count + per-state breakdown.

argv: set -e; echo 'Total:'; conntrack -C; echo; echo 'Per-state:'; conntrack -L 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1,$4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20

actions.title: conntrack -L (capped)
actions.summary: List the first 1000 connection-tracking entries. Use to see what's NAT'd right now.
actions.description: List the first 1000 connection-tracking entries. Use to see what's NAT'd right now.

actions.title: ip rule show
actions.summary: Show policy routing rules — which routing table is used for which traffic.
actions.description: Show policy routing rules — which routing table is used for which traffic.

actions.title: iptables -I INPUT -s <ip> -j DROP
actions.summary: Insert a DROP rule for one source IP at the top of the INPUT chain. Use during an active incident to immediately block a known-bad source. Rule is not persistent — survives only until iptables service reload or reboot. Combine with iptables-save to persist.
actions.description: Insert a DROP rule for one source IP at the top of the INPUT chain. Use during an active incident to immediately block a known-bad source. Rule is not persistent — survives only until iptables service reload or reboot. Combine with iptables-save to persist.
side_effects: Traffic from that IP dropped immediately.
Rule is in-memory; lost on iptables restart unless saved.

actions.title: iptables -L -nv (filter table)
actions.summary: List all filter-table rules with packet + byte counters.
actions.description: List all filter-table rules with packet + byte counters.

actions.title: iptables -F <chain>
actions.summary: Flush all rules from a single chain. ALL rules in that chain are removed; default policy still applies. INPUT/FORWARD with a DROP default plus a flushed chain means ALL traffic is dropped — only use during planned firewall rebuilds with console access ready.
actions.description: Flush all rules from a single chain. ALL rules in that chain are removed; default policy still applies. INPUT/FORWARD with a DROP default plus a flushed chain means ALL traffic is dropped — only use during planned firewall rebuilds with console access ready.
side_effects: All rules in the named chain removed.
If default policy is DROP, traffic immediately blocked.
Not persistent — undone by iptables service reload from saved rules.

args.name: chain
args.description: Chain name (INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD, or a custom chain).

actions.title: iptables -L -nv (mangle table)
actions.summary: List all mangle-table rules — QoS marks, TTL tweaks.
actions.description: List all mangle-table rules — QoS marks, TTL tweaks.

actions.title: iptables -L -nv (nat table)
actions.summary: List all nat-table rules — DNAT/SNAT/MASQUERADE.
actions.description: List all nat-table rules — DNAT/SNAT/MASQUERADE.

actions.title: iptables -D INPUT -s <ip> -j DROP
actions.summary: Remove a previously-inserted DROP rule for one IP. Use to undo a manual block. Matches the rule by literal -s/-j signature; if the rule isn't found, iptables errors and nothing changes.
actions.description: Remove a previously-inserted DROP rule for one IP. Use to undo a manual block. Matches the rule by literal -s/-j signature; if the rule isn't found, iptables errors and nothing changes.
side_effects: One matching DROP rule removed (or error if no match).
Traffic from that IP can flow again, subject to other rules.

actions.title: nft list ruleset
actions.summary: Show the full nftables ruleset across all families.
actions.description: Show the full nftables ruleset across all families.

actions.title: tc -s qdisc show
actions.summary: List active traffic-control disciplines per interface + their counters. Use to see traffic shaping in effect.
actions.description: List active traffic-control disciplines per interface + their counters. Use to see traffic shaping in effect.

argv: set -e; echo 'Total:'; conntrack -C; echo; echo 'Per-state:'; conntrack -L 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1,$4}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20

packs.name: FRRouting (FRR)
packs.description: Query the local FRRouting daemons via vtysh — BGP summary + neighbors, BFD peers, IP route summary, and interface state — plus one gated mutation: shut a BGP neighbor to drain a node from an anycast VIP. Reads are `show` commands; the drain is policy-gated (risk: high).

actions.title: vtysh -c "show bfd peers"
actions.summary: List BFD peer sessions — local/remote state (Up/Down), last diagnostic, and timers.
actions.description: List BFD peer sessions — local/remote state (Up/Down), last diagnostic, and timers.

actions.title: vtysh -c "router bgp <asn>" -c "neighbor <peer> shutdown"
actions.summary: Administratively shut a BGP neighbor session via vtysh, withdrawing every prefix this node advertises to that peer — the governed way to drain a node from an anycast VIP so the upstream ECMP routes around it within a BFD interval. Reversible: re-advertise with `no neighbor <peer> shutdown` once the node is healthy. The change is to the running config only (not persisted), so a daemon restart re-advertises. Pair with frr.bgp_summary to confirm PfxSnt drops to 0 after the drain.
actions.description: Administratively shut a BGP neighbor session via vtysh, withdrawing every prefix this node advertises to that peer — the governed way to drain a node from an anycast VIP so the upstream ECMP routes around it within a BFD interval. Reversible: re-advertise with `no neighbor <peer> shutdown` once the node is healthy. The change is to the running config only (not persisted), so a daemon restart re-advertises. Pair with frr.bgp_summary to confirm PfxSnt drops to 0 after the drain.
side_effects: Shuts the BGP session to the named peer; this node withdraws every prefix it advertises to it (often just an anycast /32 — frr.bgp_summary PfxSnt shows the count).
For an anycast VIP, upstream ECMP drops this node within a BFD interval and traffic shifts to the remaining advertisers.
Running-config only — not written to startup-config, so an FRR restart or reboot re-advertises.
Reversible with `no neighbor <peer> shutdown` (re-advertise when the node is healthy again).

args.name: asn
args.description: Local BGP autonomous system number (the `router bgp <asn>` this neighbor lives under).

args.name: peer
args.description: Neighbor to shut — an IPv4/IPv6 address, peer-group, or interface name.

examples.title: Drain this node from the anycast VIP

actions.title: vtysh -c "show bgp neighbors"
actions.summary: Show detailed BGP neighbor state — capabilities, timers, and message + prefix counters per peer.
actions.description: Show detailed BGP neighbor state — capabilities, timers, and message + prefix counters per peer.

actions.title: vtysh -c "show bgp summary"
actions.summary: Show BGP neighbor summary — per-peer state/uptime and prefix counts (PfxRcd / PfxSnt). The quick "are my BGP sessions established and exchanging routes?" check.
actions.description: Show BGP neighbor summary — per-peer state/uptime and prefix counts (PfxRcd / PfxSnt). The quick "are my BGP sessions established and exchanging routes?" check.

actions.title: vtysh -c "show interface brief"
actions.summary: Show interface brief — admin/protocol status, addresses, and VRF per interface as FRR sees them.
actions.description: Show interface brief — admin/protocol status, addresses, and VRF per interface as FRR sees them.

actions.title: vtysh -c "show ip route summary"
actions.summary: Show IP route table summary — route and FIB counts grouped by source protocol.
actions.description: Show IP route table summary — route and FIB counts grouped by source protocol.

packs.name: Filesystem search and inspection
packs.description: Generic filesystem operations an LLM-driven runner needs constantly: bounded find, recursive grep, file head/tail/hash, du, ls -la, stat. All read-only. Paths are validated against simple patterns; the runner's symlink-containment + audit redaction layers still apply on top.

actions.title: wc -l <file>
actions.summary: Count lines in one file.
actions.description: Count lines in one file.

actions.title: du -sh — top N subdirs
actions.summary: Show the top 20 immediate subdirs of one path by size.
actions.description: Show the top 20 immediate subdirs of one path by size.

actions.title: file <path>
actions.summary: Show the libmagic-detected type of one file (e.g. "ELF 64-bit executable", "ASCII text").
actions.description: Show the libmagic-detected type of one file (e.g. "ELF 64-bit executable", "ASCII text").
side_effects: One read of the first few KB of the file.

actions.title: find -name <glob> under <path>
actions.summary: Find files matching one shell glob under one path. Capped at 1000 results.
actions.description: Find files matching one shell glob under one path. Capped at 1000 results.

actions.title: Files larger than N MB
actions.summary: Find the top files by size under one path. Use to find disk-pressure culprits.
actions.description: Find the top files by size under one path. Use to find disk-pressure culprits.

argv: find {{ args.path }} -xdev \( -path /etc/ssh -o -path /etc/ssl/private -o -path /root/.ssh -o -path /etc/sudoers.d \) -prune -o -type f -size +{{ args.min_mb }}M -printf '%s %p\n' 2>/dev/null | sort -rn | head -50

actions.title: Files modified in last N minutes
actions.summary: Find files under one path modified within the last N minutes. Useful for "what just changed?".
actions.description: Find files under one path modified within the last N minutes. Useful for "what just changed?".

actions.title: setuid + setgid files
actions.summary: Find files with setuid or setgid bits. Audit for privilege escalation surface.
actions.description: Find files with setuid or setgid bits. Audit for privilege escalation surface.

actions.title: World-writable files
actions.summary: Find files with the "world-writable" bit set under one path. Security audit.
actions.description: Find files with the "world-writable" bit set under one path. Security audit.

actions.title: grep <pattern> <file>
actions.summary: Show matching lines in one file. Pattern is treated as POSIX BRE.
actions.description: Show matching lines in one file. Pattern is treated as POSIX BRE.

argv: if [ "{{ args.case_insensitive }}" = "true" ]; then grep -i -E "$P" "{{ args.path }}" | head -2000; else grep -E "$P" "{{ args.path }}" | head -2000; fi

actions.title: grep -rE <pattern> <path>
actions.summary: Search recursively under one path. Bounded to 500 matches.
actions.description: Search recursively under one path. Bounded to 500 matches.
side_effects: Recursive file-system traversal + read of matched files.

actions.title: head -n <N> <file>
actions.summary: Show the first N lines of one file.
actions.description: Show the first N lines of one file.

actions.title: ls -la <path>
actions.summary: List directory contents in long format.
actions.description: List directory contents in long format.

actions.title: sha256sum <file>
actions.summary: Show the SHA-256 of one file. Use to confirm a binary matches a known-good hash.
actions.description: Show the SHA-256 of one file. Use to confirm a binary matches a known-good hash.

actions.title: stat <path>
actions.summary: Show inode + timestamps + permissions for one path.
actions.description: Show inode + timestamps + permissions for one path.

actions.title: tail -n <N> <file>
actions.summary: Show the last N lines of one file.
actions.description: Show the last N lines of one file.

actions.description: Show per-line authorship for one file. Path is relative to repo root.

actions.title: git branch -av

actions.description: List all branches (local + remote-tracking) with last commit.

actions.title: git diff HEAD
actions.summary: Show uncommitted changes against HEAD. Capped at 1MB of output.
actions.description: Show uncommitted changes against HEAD. Capped at 1MB of output.

actions.description: List the last N commits with author + date + subject.

actions.description: Show local HEAD-movement history. Use to find recent rebases / resets.

actions.description: List the remote URLs (fetch + push).

actions.description: Show the commit message + diff for one commit ref.

actions.description: Show working-tree status — uncommitted changes, branch, ahead/behind.

packs.name: GitHub CLI operations
packs.description: GitHub introspection — PRs, issues, repos, workflow runs, releases, commit checks, search — plus operator actions: merge PR, close PR, rerun workflow, dispatch workflow. Authenticates via the runner host's `gh auth status` token (i.e. ~/.config/gh).

actions.title: gh auth status

packs.name: iperf3 network throughput
packs.description: Active network throughput measurement with iperf3 — TCP bandwidth and UDP jitter / packet loss — between two nodes in the fleet. Run the one-shot server on one runner, then probe it with the client from another. Use to answer "what is the real bandwidth (or loss) between these two hosts?"

actions.title: iperf3 TCP throughput test
actions.summary: Measure TCP throughput to a target host running an iperf3 server (`iperf3 -s`).
actions.description: Measure TCP throughput to a target host running an iperf3 server (`iperf3 -s`). Reports bits/sec, retransmits, and per-stream detail as JSON. Use to answer "what is the real bandwidth between these two hosts?" — e.g. diagnosing slow replication, backups, or a suspect link. Set reverse=true to measure the download direction (server → runner). The target MUST already be running an iperf3 server; the test generates sustained traffic for `duration` seconds and can saturate a shared link, so cap it with `bitrate` and keep `duration` short on production paths.
side_effects: Opens a TCP control + data connection to the target's iperf3 server (default port 5201).
Generates sustained traffic at up to line rate (unless `bitrate` caps it) for the test duration — can saturate a shared link.
Read-only on both hosts; no state is changed.

args.name: host
args.description: Target host (hostname, IPv4, or IPv6) running an iperf3 server.

args.name: bitrate
args.description: Target bitrate cap in bits/sec, optional K/M/G suffix (e.g. "500M"). "0" means unlimited (measure true capacity).

args.name: reverse
args.description: Reverse mode — the server sends and the runner receives (measures the download direction).

examples.title: Download direction, 4 streams, capped at 500 Mbit

actions.title: iperf3 one-shot server
actions.summary: Run a one-shot iperf3 server: listen on a port, accept a single throughput test from one client, print the server-side result as JSON, and exit.
actions.description: Run a one-shot iperf3 server: listen on a port, accept a single throughput test from one client, print the server-side result as JSON, and exit. Pair it with iperf3.client / iperf3.udp on another node — start the server here, then run the client there within the listen window. One server handles both TCP and UDP clients. It self-terminates after idle_timeout seconds if no client connects (and is bounded by the action timeout), so it never leaves a lingering daemon or open port. Optionally restrict the listener to a single interface with `bind`.
side_effects: Opens an inbound listening port (default 5201) for up to idle_timeout seconds.
Accepts ONE throughput test from a connecting client and discards the data; self-closes after that test or on idle timeout.
Opens the host to inbound connections on that port while it waits — anyone who can reach it may run a test through it or occupy the one-off slot.
Changes no host state and leaves no daemon running — the listener is transient and self-closing.

args.name: idle_timeout
args.description: Seconds to wait for a client before giving up (also the stuck-guard during a test). The server exits after this many idle seconds if no client connects. Maps to iperf3 --idle-timeout.

args.name: bind
args.description: Restrict the listener to one interface by address (e.g. "10.0.0.5"). Empty (the default) listens on all interfaces.

examples.title: Listen on 5201 for one test, give up after 30s idle
Listen only on the private interface

actions.title: iperf3 UDP jitter + loss test
actions.summary: Measure UDP throughput, jitter, and packet loss to a target host running an iperf3 server (`iperf3 -s`). Sends a UDP stream at the target `bitrate` and reports jitter (ms) and lost-packet percentage as JSON. Use to check a path for loss or jitter (VoIP, video, real-time links) or to probe how much a link drops at a given rate. Set reverse=true to test the download direction (server → runner). The target MUST already be running an iperf3 server; generates sustained UDP traffic for `duration` seconds.
actions.description: Measure UDP throughput, jitter, and packet loss to a target host running an iperf3 server (`iperf3 -s`). Sends a UDP stream at the target `bitrate` and reports jitter (ms) and lost-packet percentage as JSON. Use to check a path for loss or jitter (VoIP, video, real-time links) or to probe how much a link drops at a given rate. Set reverse=true to test the download direction (server → runner). The target MUST already be running an iperf3 server; generates sustained UDP traffic for `duration` seconds.
side_effects: Sends a UDP stream to the target's iperf3 server (default port 5201) at the target bitrate.
Generates sustained UDP traffic for the test duration — can congest a shared link.
Read-only on both hosts; no state is changed.

args.name: host
args.description: Target host (hostname, IPv4, or IPv6) running an iperf3 server.

args.name: bitrate
args.description: Target UDP bitrate in bits/sec, optional K/M/G suffix (e.g. "50M"). This is the rate the sender aims for; the report shows how much of it arrived.

args.name: reverse
args.description: Reverse mode — the server sends and the runner receives (measures the download direction).

actions.title: iperf3 version + build info
actions.summary: Show the installed iperf3 version and build info (cJSON / OpenSSL). Read-only local check with no network — use it to confirm the runner actually has iperf3 before dispatching a throughput test.
actions.description: Show the installed iperf3 version and build info (cJSON / OpenSSL). Read-only local check with no network — use it to confirm the runner actually has iperf3 before dispatching a throughput test.

packs.name: iSCSI initiator
packs.description: Inspect the host's iSCSI initiator (open-iscsi): active sessions, per-session detail (targets, connections, negotiated parameters), configured target nodes, and initiator interfaces. Read-only.

actions.title: iscsiadm -m iface
actions.summary: List initiator interfaces (the iface bindings sessions run over).
actions.description: List initiator interfaces (the iface bindings sessions run over).

actions.title: iscsiadm -m node
actions.summary: List configured / discovered target nodes in the initiator database.
actions.description: List configured / discovered target nodes in the initiator database.

actions.title: iscsiadm -m session -P 3
actions.summary: Show full per-session detail at print level 3 — target IQN, portal, session and connection state, negotiated parameters, and the attached SCSI disks.
actions.description: Show full per-session detail at print level 3 — target IQN, portal, session and connection state, negotiated parameters, and the attached SCSI disks.

actions.title: iscsiadm -m session
actions.summary: List active iSCSI sessions — one line per session (transport, target IQN, sid). Returns nothing and succeeds when there are no active sessions (iscsiadm exits 21 in that case, which is treated as success, not failure).
actions.description: List active iSCSI sessions — one line per session (transport, target IQN, sid). Returns nothing and succeeds when there are no active sessions (iscsiadm exits 21 in that case, which is treated as success, not failure).

packs.name: JVM introspection
packs.description: JVM diagnostics via jcmd/jstack/jmap/jstat/JFR. Requires runner uid to match the JVM process owner (or CAP_SYS_PTRACE) — actions return a permission error cleanly when uid doesn't match.

actions.title: jcmd VM.classloader_stats
actions.summary: Show per-classloader statistics — instance count, loaded classes, and chunk/block bytes. Use to spot a class-loader leak. Uses the attach API, so it works on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) where jvm.jstat_class fails.
actions.description: Show per-classloader statistics — instance count, loaded classes, and chunk/block bytes. Use to spot a class-loader leak. Uses the attach API, so it works on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) where jvm.jstat_class fails.

actions.title: jmap -dump:live (heap dump)
actions.summary: Triggers a full GC, then dumps the live heap to /tmp/<pid>-heap.hprof. Can be many GB. Causes a long STW pause (seconds). Use only when you have analyst capacity to load the .hprof and disk space to hold it.
actions.description: Triggers a full GC, then dumps the live heap to /tmp/<pid>-heap.hprof. Can be many GB. Causes a long STW pause (seconds). Use only when you have analyst capacity to load the .hprof and disk space to hold it.
side_effects: JVM pause for the duration of the dump (seconds to minutes).

actions.title: jcmd GC.heap_info
actions.summary: Show heap geometry — used/committed per generation (Eden, Survivor, Old, Metaspace) via the attach API. Works on JDK 9+ where `jmap -heap` was removed, and on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (e.g. Cassandra).
actions.description: Show heap geometry — used/committed per generation (Eden, Survivor, Old, Metaspace) via the attach API. Works on JDK 9+ where `jmap -heap` was removed, and on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (e.g. Cassandra).

actions.title: jcmd <pid> GC.run
actions.summary: Trigger a full GC. Use to compare heap usage before/after, or to force collection ahead of a heap dump for cleaner output. Causes a pause whose length depends on heap size + collector tuning.
actions.description: Trigger a full GC. Use to compare heap usage before/after, or to force collection ahead of a heap dump for cleaner output. Causes a pause whose length depends on heap size + collector tuning.
side_effects: Pause of seconds on large heaps.

actions.title: Dump active JFR recording
actions.summary: Dumps the active "emisar" recording to its current file. Use mid-flight to grab a snapshot without stopping the recording.
actions.description: Dumps the active "emisar" recording to its current file. Use mid-flight to grab a snapshot without stopping the recording.

actions.title: Start a Java Flight Recording
actions.summary: Starts a 60-second Java Flight Recording named "emisar". JFR is low-overhead (~1%). Pair with `jvm.jfr_dump` to retrieve the recording, then `jvm.jfr_stop` to end it.
actions.description: Starts a 60-second Java Flight Recording named "emisar". JFR is low-overhead (~1%). Pair with `jvm.jfr_dump` to retrieve the recording, then `jvm.jfr_stop` to end it.
side_effects: Starts a JFR session in the JVM.

actions.title: Stop active JFR recording
actions.summary: Stops the "emisar" recording and finalizes the .jfr file.
actions.description: Stops the "emisar" recording and finalizes the .jfr file.

actions.title: jmap -histo:live (top 50)
actions.summary: List top classes by retained-heap size after a forced full GC. Use to find the leak candidate.
actions.description: List top classes by retained-heap size after a forced full GC. Use to find the leak candidate.
side_effects: JVM pause for the duration of the GC.

actions.title: jstack -l (thread dump)
actions.summary: Dump full thread state with lock info. The canonical "what are all my threads doing?" diagnostic.
actions.description: Dump full thread state with lock info. The canonical "what are all my threads doing?" diagnostic.
side_effects: Read-only; introduces a STW pause on the JVM (~ms).

actions.title: jstack filtered to BLOCKED threads
actions.summary: Runs jstack and filters output to threads in BLOCKED state plus their lock owners. Use to find deadlocks fast.
actions.description: Runs jstack and filters output to threads in BLOCKED state plus their lock owners. Use to find deadlocks fast.

argv: jstack -l {{ args.pid }} | awk '/^"/ {hdr=$0; next} /java.lang.Thread.State: BLOCKED/ {print hdr; print; flag=1; next} flag && /^$/ {flag=0; next} flag {print}'

actions.title: jstat -class
actions.summary: Show class-loader stats — loaded class count and bytes. Use to spot a class-loading leak. Needs jvmstat perf data: fails with "<pid> not found" on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use jvm.jmap_histo_live (live-object histogram) or JFR instead.
actions.description: Show class-loader stats — loaded class count and bytes. Use to spot a class-loading leak. Needs jvmstat perf data: fails with "<pid> not found" on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use jvm.jmap_histo_live (live-object histogram) or JFR instead.

actions.title: jstat -gc (10 samples)
actions.summary: Show ten 1-second samples of GC stats. Surfaces young + old GC time, survivor sizes, metaspace usage. Use to spot a GC storm. Needs jvmstat perf data: fails with "<pid> not found" on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use jvm.heap_summary for heap state, or JFR (jvm.jfr_start / jvm.jfr_dump) for GC behavior over time.
actions.description: Show ten 1-second samples of GC stats. Surfaces young + old GC time, survivor sizes, metaspace usage. Use to spot a GC storm. Needs jvmstat perf data: fails with "<pid> not found" on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use jvm.heap_summary for heap state, or JFR (jvm.jfr_start / jvm.jfr_dump) for GC behavior over time.

actions.title: jcmd PerfCounter.print
actions.summary: Show JVM performance counters — class loading, JIT, GC. Cheaper than jstat for one-shot. Reads the same jvmstat perf data as jstat: fails on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use the attach-based reads (jvm.heap_summary, jvm.vm_flags, jvm.jstack) or JFR.
actions.description: Show JVM performance counters — class loading, JIT, GC. Cheaper than jstat for one-shot. Reads the same jvmstat perf data as jstat: fails on JVMs running -XX:+PerfDisableSharedMem (the Cassandra default) — there use the attach-based reads (jvm.heap_summary, jvm.vm_flags, jvm.jstack) or JFR.

actions.title: OS thread count for a JVM
actions.summary: Counts /proc/<pid>/task entries — fastest way to spot "do we have an unbounded thread pool?"
actions.description: Counts /proc/<pid>/task entries — fastest way to spot "do we have an unbounded thread pool?"

actions.title: jcmd VM.flags
actions.summary: Returns the JVM command-line and tuning flags. Use to confirm "is -Xmx set to what we expect?"
actions.description: Returns the JVM command-line and tuning flags. Use to confirm "is -Xmx set to what we expect?"

actions.title: jcmd VM.system_properties
actions.summary: Dump every -Dkey=value system property the JVM was started with (jcmd VM.system_properties = System.getProperties()).
actions.description: Dump every -Dkey=value system property the JVM was started with (jcmd VM.system_properties = System.getProperties()). Use to confirm config overrides took effect — but note this deliberately surfaces every launch -D property, which routinely carries secrets (spring.datasource.password, javax.net.ssl.keyStorePassword, JDBC / cloud credentials). Scope it by policy. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but exposes every -D launch property (may include secrets).

actions.title: jcmd VM.uptime
actions.summary: Show how long this JVM has been running.
actions.description: Show how long this JVM has been running.

argv: jstack -l {{ args.pid }} | awk '/^"/ {hdr=$0; next} /java.lang.Thread.State: BLOCKED/ {print hdr; print; flag=1; next} flag && /^$/ {flag=0; next} flag {print}'

actions.title: OS thread count for a JVM

packs.name: Kafka cluster operations
packs.description: Topic introspection, consumer-group lag, broker config, ACLs, and narrow mutators (reset_offsets, alter_topic_retention, delete_consumer_group, preferred_leader_election). Auth via KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP env var on the runner host plus optional KAFKA_COMMAND_CONFIG (jaas / SASL / SSL).

actions.title: Set topic retention.ms
actions.summary: Sets `retention.ms` on a topic. Lower values cause old data to be deleted in the next log roll.
actions.description: Sets `retention.ms` on a topic. Lower values cause old data to be deleted in the next log roll.
side_effects: Old segments past retention will be deleted on next log roll.
Replication lag may briefly spike during cleanup.

actions.title: Broker API versions
actions.summary: Show the API protocol versions supported by each broker. Useful to confirm rolling upgrade.
actions.description: Show the API protocol versions supported by each broker. Useful to confirm rolling upgrade.

actions.title: Broker configs
actions.summary: List all broker-level configs (dynamic + static + defaults).
actions.description: List all broker-level configs (dynamic + static + defaults).

actions.title: Cluster metadata
actions.summary: Show broker IDs, controller, cluster ID. Read-only.
actions.description: Show broker IDs, controller, cluster ID. Read-only.

actions.title: Consumer lag for a group
actions.summary: Show per-partition lag (log-end-offset minus committed offset) for one consumer group. The canonical "is consumption keeping up?" check.
actions.description: Show per-partition lag (log-end-offset minus committed offset) for one consumer group. The canonical "is consumption keeping up?" check.

actions.title: Delete consumer group
actions.summary: Removes a consumer group from the coordinator. The group must be empty (no active members).
actions.description: Removes a consumer group from the coordinator. The group must be empty (no active members).
side_effects: Group must have no active members; command fails otherwise.
Committed offsets for the group are dropped.

actions.title: Describe consumer group
actions.summary: Show member list, partition assignment, host for one consumer group.
actions.description: Show member list, partition assignment, host for one consumer group.

actions.title: Describe topic
actions.summary: Show partition layout, leaders, ISR, replicas for one topic.
actions.description: Show partition layout, leaders, ISR, replicas for one topic.

actions.title: Partition leaders by broker
actions.summary: Count partitions where each broker is leader. Uneven distribution → preferred-leader election.
actions.description: Count partitions where each broker is leader. Uneven distribution → preferred-leader election.

argv: kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server "$KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP" --describe | awk '/Leader/ {match($0,/Leader: [0-9]+/); if (RSTART) print substr($0,RSTART+8,RLENGTH-8)}' | sort | uniq -c

actions.title: List ACLs
actions.summary: List every ACL binding on the cluster.
actions.description: List every ACL binding on the cluster.

actions.title: Committed offsets per partition
actions.summary: Show the same view as consumer_lag but emphasizes committed-offset reading. Useful before reset_offsets.
actions.description: Show the same view as consumer_lag but emphasizes committed-offset reading. Useful before reset_offsets.

actions.title: List consumer groups
actions.summary: List all consumer groups in the cluster.
actions.description: List all consumer groups in the cluster.

actions.title: List topics
actions.summary: List all topics in the cluster.
actions.description: List all topics in the cluster.

actions.title: Trigger preferred-leader election
actions.summary: Forces preferred-leader election cluster-wide. Use after a broker restart leaves leaders skewed.
actions.description: Forces preferred-leader election cluster-wide. Use after a broker restart leaves leaders skewed.
side_effects: Brief leadership transitions; in-flight produce/consume may retry.
May cause a temporary spike in metadata churn.

actions.title: Partition reassignment status
actions.summary: Show the status of any in-flight partition reassignments.
actions.description: Show the status of any in-flight partition reassignments.

actions.title: Reset group offsets to earliest
actions.summary: Rewinds a consumer group's offsets to the start of every partition. Causes a full replay — can be hours to days of duplicate processing.
actions.description: Rewinds a consumer group's offsets to the start of every partition. Causes a full replay — can be hours to days of duplicate processing.
side_effects: Consumer group must be stopped first; this command fails if still active.
Group will re-process every message in the topic from the beginning.

actions.title: Reset group offsets to latest (skip all)
actions.summary: Fast-forwards a consumer group past every existing message — they will NOT be processed. Data loss for that consumer.
actions.description: Fast-forwards a consumer group past every existing message — they will NOT be processed. Data loss for that consumer.
side_effects: Consumer group must be stopped first; this command fails if still active.
All unconsumed messages will be skipped.

actions.title: Topic configs

actions.title: Unavailable partitions

actions.description: List partitions with no leader. These mean writes are failing.

packs.name: Kubernetes operations pack
packs.description: Operator pack for Kubernetes clusters: discovery (pods, nodes, services, deployments), deep introspection (describe, logs, events), rollouts (status, history, restart, undo), and narrow node/pod mutators (cordon, drain, delete). Cluster targeting via KUBECONFIG env var on the runner host; context selectable per call.

actions.summary: Lists API versions available on the cluster — useful for "does this k8s version support …?" checks.
actions.description: Lists API versions available on the cluster — useful for "does this k8s version support …?" checks.

actions.title: List my RBAC permissions (auth can-i --list)
actions.summary: `kubectl auth can-i --list` — every action the kubeconfig identity is allowed, optionally scoped to one namespace. Confirms whether the runner's identity can perform the pack's mutators (drain, scale, delete). Read-only.
actions.description: `kubectl auth can-i --list` — every action the kubeconfig identity is allowed, optionally scoped to one namespace. Confirms whether the runner's identity can perform the pack's mutators (drain, scale, delete). Read-only.

actions.title: Delete one pod (force-restart-by-killing)
actions.summary: Deletes one pod by name. Use to force-restart a single pod (the controller will recreate it). Faster than rollout_restart for a one-off bad pod. Namespace required to prevent wildcard mistakes.
actions.description: Deletes one pod by name. Use to force-restart a single pod (the controller will recreate it). Faster than rollout_restart for a one-off bad pod. Namespace required to prevent wildcard mistakes.
side_effects: Pod sent SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
Controller recreates the pod unless it was a standalone Pod.

actions.title: kubectl drain node (cordon + evict)
actions.summary: Cordons the node and evicts every pod that has a controller (Deployment/StatefulSet/etc). DaemonSets are ignored and emptyDir data is deleted with --delete-emptydir-data. Use before scheduled maintenance.
actions.description: Cordons the node and evicts every pod that has a controller (Deployment/StatefulSet/etc). DaemonSets are ignored and emptyDir data is deleted with --delete-emptydir-data. Use before scheduled maintenance.
side_effects: Every controller-managed pod evicted; recreated elsewhere.
emptyDir volumes on this node are lost.

actions.title: kubectl rollout restart (rolling)
actions.summary: Triggers a rolling restart — pods are replaced one-by-one respecting maxSurge/maxUnavailable. In-flight requests on terminated pods are gracefully drained per pod terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
actions.description: Triggers a rolling restart — pods are replaced one-by-one respecting maxSurge/maxUnavailable. In-flight requests on terminated pods are gracefully drained per pod terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
side_effects: Triggers a fresh rollout of the resource.
Pods are replaced one-by-one; in-flight requests are drained per pod.

argv: kubectl ${KCTX:+--context=$KCTX} -n {{ args.namespace }} rollout restart {{ args.kind }}/{{ args.name }}

actions.description: Rolls back to the previous revision. Bring `rollout_history` first to confirm what "previous" means in this case.

Pods replaced one-by-one; previous container image becomes current.

argv: kubectl ${KCTX:+--context=$KCTX} -n {{ args.namespace }} rollout undo {{ args.kind }}/{{ args.name }}
