actions.title: nomad job inspect <id>
actions.summary: Dump one job's full spec as JSON. This surfaces the job's `env` and `template` blocks, which routinely carry injected secrets (DB URLs, API keys, rendered Vault templates). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
actions.description: Dump one job's full spec as JSON. This surfaces the job's `env` and `template` blocks, which routinely carry injected secrets (DB URLs, API keys, rendered Vault templates). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but exposes the job's env/template blocks (may include secrets).

args.name: job

actions.title: nomad job inspect <id> (resource summary)
actions.summary: List the CPU and memory reservation and the replica count for every task group and task in a job — the compact read companion to nomad.task_resources_set, so you can see current limits before vertical-scaling. Projects only the resource fields from the full jobspec: per task the CPU (MHz), cores, MemoryMB, and MemoryMaxMB, and per group the count.
actions.description: List the CPU and memory reservation and the replica count for every task group and task in a job — the compact read companion to nomad.task_resources_set, so you can see current limits before vertical-scaling. Projects only the resource fields from the full jobspec: per task the CPU (MHz), cores, MemoryMB, and MemoryMaxMB, and per group the count.
side_effects: Read-only — never writes or mutates job state.

actions.title: nomad job revert <id> <version>

actions.description: Reverts a job to a prior version. Equivalent to re-submitting that version.

Triggers a rolling update (per the new/old spec's update stanza).

actions.description: Adjusts the count for one task group.
side_effects: Scheduler creates or stops allocations to reach the target count.
Stopped allocations follow the kill_timeout / shutdown_delay stanzas.

actions.description: List all jobs with their type, priority, status, and submit time.

History retained unless --purge is used (this action does NOT purge).

actions.title: GET /v1/status/leader

actions.description: Enables drain mode on one node. Allocations migrate; new ones are blocked.

May take minutes to complete depending on workload.

args.name: deadline
args.description: Force-eject after this duration if migration hasn't completed.

actions.title: nomad node drain -disable
actions.summary: Disables drain on one node. The node becomes eligible again (assuming eligibility wasn't separately disabled).
actions.description: Disables drain on one node. The node becomes eligible again (assuming eligibility wasn't separately disabled).
side_effects: Node may immediately receive new allocations.

actions.summary: Marks one node ineligible for new allocations. Existing allocations are not migrated.
actions.description: Marks one node ineligible for new allocations. Existing allocations are not migrated.

actions.title: nomad node eligibility -enable
actions.summary: Re-enables a node for new allocations.
actions.description: Re-enables a node for new allocations.
side_effects: Node may immediately receive new allocations.

actions.title: Nodes with eligibility != eligible
actions.summary: List nodes that are ineligible for new allocations (drained or manually disabled).
actions.description: List nodes that are ineligible for new allocations (drained or manually disabled).

actions.title: Purge a dead node (PUT /v1/node/<id>/purge)
actions.summary: Removes a dead (down) node from the catalog; allocations on it are GC'd. There is no `nomad node purge` CLI subcommand — this is the HTTP API (PUT /v1/node/<id>/purge). Only valid for nodes that are down.
actions.description: Removes a dead (down) node from the catalog; allocations on it are GC'd. There is no `nomad node purge` CLI subcommand — this is the HTTP API (PUT /v1/node/<id>/purge). Only valid for nodes that are down.
side_effects: Node entry removed permanently (one API PUT).
Only valid for nodes that are down — running nodes refuse purge.

actions.title: nomad node status
actions.summary: List all clients (nodes) with status, datacenter, drain state, eligibility.
actions.description: List all clients (nodes) with status, datacenter, drain state, eligibility.

actions.description: Show one node's full detail — resources, allocations, events, drivers.

actions.description: Show the autopilot configuration (dead-server cleanup, redundancy zones, server stabilization).

actions.description: Show the autopilot health view — leader health, follower lag, server stabilization.

actions.title: nomad operator raft list-peers

actions.description: List the Raft peers — voter status, suffrage, address.

actions.summary: Forcibly removes a server from Raft. Use ONLY when a server is permanently gone and autopilot hasn't cleaned it up.
actions.description: Forcibly removes a server from Raft. Use ONLY when a server is permanently gone and autopilot hasn't cleaned it up.
side_effects: Wrong target = lost quorum / split brain.

actions.title: nomad operator scheduler get-config
actions.summary: Show the cluster's scheduler configuration — the scheduler algorithm (binpack/spread), memory oversubscription, preemption settings (system/batch/ service/sysbatch), job-registration rejection, and eval-broker pause state. This is the "why is placement behaving this way / is preemption on" read for incident triage. Read-only; requires a Nomad token with the operator:read capability.
actions.description: Show the cluster's scheduler configuration — the scheduler algorithm (binpack/spread), memory oversubscription, preemption settings (system/batch/ service/sysbatch), job-registration rejection, and eval-broker pause state. This is the "why is placement behaving this way / is preemption on" read for incident triage. Read-only; requires a Nomad token with the operator:read capability.

actions.title: nomad quota list
actions.summary: List resource quotas (Enterprise feature).
actions.description: List resource quotas (Enterprise feature).

actions.title: nomad server members (json)
actions.summary: List the server members as JSON. Use for programmatic consumption.
actions.description: List the server members as JSON. Use for programmatic consumption.

actions.title: nomad service info <name>
actions.summary: Show the live instances of one service in Nomad's native service discovery (no Consul) — each instance's address, port, node, and allocation ID. This is the "where is this service running, on what address" read for a registered service name (list them with nomad.service_list). Requires a Nomad token with the read-job capability on the namespace.
actions.description: Show the live instances of one service in Nomad's native service discovery (no Consul) — each instance's address, port, node, and allocation ID. This is the "where is this service running, on what address" read for a registered service name (list them with nomad.service_list). Requires a Nomad token with the read-job capability on the namespace.

actions.summary: List the services registered in Nomad's native service discovery (no Consul) — the registered service names and their tags in the current namespace. This is the "what services does Nomad know about" read; use nomad.service_info to see the live instances (address, port, node, alloc) behind one service. Requires a Nomad token with the read-job capability on the namespace.
actions.description: List the services registered in Nomad's native service discovery (no Consul) — the registered service names and their tags in the current namespace. This is the "what services does Nomad know about" read; use nomad.service_info to see the live instances (address, port, node, alloc) behind one service. Requires a Nomad token with the read-job capability on the namespace.

actions.title: nomad system gc
actions.summary: Forces a system-wide GC of jobs, allocations, evaluations, and deployments past their GC threshold.
actions.description: Forces a system-wide GC of jobs, allocations, evaluations, and deployments past their GC threshold.
side_effects: Old dead jobs/evals/allocs/deployments are removed from the catalog.

actions.title: nomad job inspect | set task CPU/memory | job run
actions.summary: Vertical-scale one task: set its CPU and/or memory limits and re-register the job. Nomad has no atomic resource-change command, so this fetches the live jobspec, patches only the named task's CPU (MHz), MemoryMB, and MemoryMaxMB, and re-registers it with an optimistic JobModifyIndex check. The cloud never supplies jobspec JSON — only the bounded ids and integers below. Pair with nomad.job_resources to read current limits first, and nomad.job_scale to change the replica count.
actions.description: Vertical-scale one task: set its CPU and/or memory limits and re-register the job. Nomad has no atomic resource-change command, so this fetches the live jobspec, patches only the named task's CPU (MHz), MemoryMB, and MemoryMaxMB, and re-registers it with an optimistic JobModifyIndex check. The cloud never supplies jobspec JSON — only the bounded ids and integers below. Pair with nomad.job_resources to read current limits first, and nomad.job_scale to change the replica count.
side_effects: Re-registers the job with the patched task resources (one read + one write API call).
Triggers a rolling update of the task group — allocations are replaced per its update stanza.
Setting memory below the task's real working set can cause OOM kills on the new allocations.
Refuses to write if the job changed since it was read (JobModifyIndex mismatch).

args.name: task
args.description: Task name. Empty selects the group's only task (errors if the group has more than one).

args.name: cpu
args.description: New CPU reservation in MHz. 0 leaves it unchanged.

args.name: memory
args.description: New memory reservation (MemoryMB). 0 leaves it unchanged.

args.name: memory_max
args.description: New memory oversubscription cap (MemoryMaxMB). 0 leaves it unchanged.

examples.title: Raise memory only on a single-task group (auto-select)

actions.title: Show Redis INFO inside an allocation (nomad alloc exec redis-cli info)
Ping Redis inside an allocation (nomad alloc exec redis-cli ping)
List a directory in an allocation (nomad fs/ls API)
Stat a path in an allocation (nomad fs/stat API)
Tail a task's application logs — stdout (nomad alloc logs)
Tail a task's application logs — stderr (nomad alloc logs -stderr)

argv: if [ -n "{{ args.task }}" ]; then nomad alloc restart -task {{ args.task }} {{ args.alloc_id }}; else nomad alloc restart {{ args.alloc_id }}; fi
if [ -n "{{ args.task }}" ]; then nomad alloc signal -s {{ args.signal }} -task {{ args.task }} {{ args.alloc_id }}; else nomad alloc signal -s {{ args.signal }} {{ args.alloc_id }}; fi
M=''; if [ -n '{{ args.meta_kv }}' ]; then for kv in $(echo '{{ args.meta_kv }}' | tr ',' ' '); do M="$M -meta $kv"; done; fi; nomad job dispatch $M {{ args.job }}

actions.title: nomad job inspect | set task CPU/memory | job run

packs.name: pfSense firewall
packs.description: Operate a pfSense firewall over its REST API: read firewall rules, NAT, aliases, virtual IPs, interface / gateway (config + status) / service / VPN status, DHCP leases, routes, ARP, CARP, pf tables, installed packages, the config-change history, and firewall / system / auth / DHCP / OpenVPN logs, plus a few gated mutators (apply filter, flush states, restart a service, reboot). Uses the community pfSense-pkg-RESTAPI package (/api/v2), which runs on the firewall and works across CE and Plus.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/aliases
actions.summary: List firewall aliases (named host / network / port groups) and their members. Use to see which IPs a block/allow list currently contains.
actions.description: List firewall aliases (named host / network / port groups) and their members. Use to see which IPs a block/allow list currently contains.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: POST /api/v2/firewall/apply
actions.summary: Apply staged firewall changes — reloads the pf filter so pending rule, NAT, or alias edits take effect. Idempotent and low blast radius, but it does push whatever is currently staged into the live ruleset.
actions.description: Apply staged firewall changes — reloads the pf filter so pending rule, NAT, or alias edits take effect. Idempotent and low blast radius, but it does push whatever is currently staged into the live ruleset.
side_effects: Applies any staged firewall configuration changes to the live ruleset.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/diagnostics/arp_table
actions.summary: Show the ARP table — IP ↔ MAC ↔ interface, with hostname and expiry. Use to confirm a host is on the network and which interface it is on.
actions.description: Show the ARP table — IP ↔ MAC ↔ interface, with hostname and expiry. Use to confirm a host is on the network and which interface it is on.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/logs/auth
actions.summary: Show recent authentication-log entries — webConfigurator and SSH logins to the firewall, successes and failures, with user and source IP. Use to spot brute-force attempts or confirm who logged in.
actions.description: Show recent authentication-log entries — webConfigurator and SSH logins to the firewall, successes and failures, with user and source IP. Use to spot brute-force attempts or confirm who logged in.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log lines to return.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/carp
actions.summary: Show CARP high-availability status — whether this node is MASTER or BACKUP and the state of each virtual IP. Use to confirm HA roles after a failover.
actions.description: Show CARP high-availability status — whether this node is MASTER or BACKUP and the state of each virtual IP. Use to confirm HA roles after a failover.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/system/certificate_authorities
actions.summary: List the certificate authorities — each CA's description, refid, parent CA (caref, for intermediates), OS-trust-store flag, and serial. The CA private key is never returned: the action selects only these non-secret fields, so the `prv` PEM is dropped at the source. (The REST API exposes no structured expiry for CAs; use pfsense.certificates for cert validity windows.)
actions.description: List the certificate authorities — each CA's description, refid, parent CA (caref, for intermediates), OS-trust-store flag, and serial. The CA private key is never returned: the action selects only these non-secret fields, so the `prv` PEM is dropped at the source. (The REST API exposes no structured expiry for CAs; use pfsense.certificates for cert validity windows.)
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/system/certificates
actions.summary: List the certificate store — each certificate's description, refid, signing CA (caref), type, and validity window (valid_from / valid_until / valid_days_left) for expiry monitoring. The private key is never returned: the action selects only these non-secret fields, so the `prv` PEM is dropped at the source.
actions.description: List the certificate store — each certificate's description, refid, signing CA (caref), type, and validity window (valid_from / valid_until / valid_days_left) for expiry monitoring. The private key is never returned: the action selects only these non-secret fields, so the `prv` PEM is dropped at the source.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/diagnostics/config_history/revisions
actions.summary: List the saved configuration revisions — the change history with each revision's time, description (what changed), and the user who made it. Use to answer "what changed on the firewall and when". Returns revision metadata only, not the full configuration.
actions.description: List the saved configuration revisions — the change history with each revision's time, description (what changed), and the user who made it. Use to answer "what changed on the firewall and when". Returns revision metadata only, not the full configuration.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/system/crls
actions.summary: List the certificate revocation lists — each CRL's description, refid, issuing CA (caref), method (internal / existing), lifetime, serial, and the count of revoked certificates. The raw CRL PEM and the full revoked-entry list are omitted to keep the output compact (neither is a secret).
actions.description: List the certificate revocation lists — each CRL's description, refid, issuing CA (caref), method (internal / existing), lifetime, serial, and the count of revoked certificates. The raw CRL PEM and the full revoked-entry list are omitted to keep the output compact (neither is a secret).
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/dhcp_server/leases
actions.summary: List DHCP leases — IP, MAC, hostname, and state (active / expired / static). Works for both the ISC and Kea backends. Use to find what a device was assigned.
actions.description: List DHCP leases — IP, MAC, hostname, and state (active / expired / static). Works for both the ISC and Kea backends. Use to find what a device was assigned.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/logs/dhcp
actions.summary: Show recent DHCP server log entries — lease discover/offer/request/ack and declines, with client MACs and assigned addresses. Use to debug a client that is not getting an address or to see who leased what.
actions.description: Show recent DHCP server log entries — lease discover/offer/request/ack and declines, with client MACs and assigned addresses. Use to debug a client that is not getting an address or to see who leased what.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log lines to return.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/logs/firewall
actions.summary: Show recent firewall filter-log entries — blocked/passed packets with time, interface, action, and source/destination. Use to see what is being dropped.
actions.description: Show recent firewall filter-log entries — blocked/passed packets with time, interface, action, and source/destination. Use to see what is being dropped.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log lines to return.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/rules
actions.summary: List all configured firewall filter rules, in order, with interface, action, source, destination, and description. Use to see what is allowed or blocked.
actions.description: List all configured firewall filter rules, in order, with interface, action, source, destination, and description. Use to see what is allowed or blocked.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: DELETE /api/v2/firewall/states
actions.summary: Flush the entire firewall state table — drops every tracked connection through the firewall. Existing sessions must re-establish. Use to clear a stuck state table or force traffic to re-evaluate against new rules.
actions.description: Flush the entire firewall state table — drops every tracked connection through the firewall. Existing sessions must re-establish. Use to clear a stuck state table or force traffic to re-evaluate against new rules.
side_effects: Clears the entire pf state table.
Every active connection through the firewall is dropped and must reconnect.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/routing/gateway/groups
actions.summary: List the configured gateway groups — the multi-WAN failover / load-balance tiers that decide which gateway carries traffic when one is down. Use to understand failover policy; pair with gateway_status for live up/down state.
actions.description: List the configured gateway groups — the multi-WAN failover / load-balance tiers that decide which gateway carries traffic when one is down. Use to understand failover policy; pair with gateway_status for live up/down state.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/gateways
actions.summary: Show gateway monitoring (dpinger) — per-gateway up/down state, RTT, std-dev, and packet loss. Use to diagnose WAN / multi-WAN failover.
actions.description: Show gateway monitoring (dpinger) — per-gateway up/down state, RTT, std-dev, and packet loss. Use to diagnose WAN / multi-WAN failover.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/routing/gateways
actions.summary: List the configured gateways (the routing config, not live status) — each gateway's name, interface, gateway/monitor IP, default flag, and weight. Use pfsense.gateway_status for live dpinger up/down/RTT, and pfsense.gateway_groups for failover groups.
actions.description: List the configured gateways (the routing config, not live status) — each gateway's name, interface, gateway/monitor IP, default flag, and weight. Use pfsense.gateway_status for live dpinger up/down/RTT, and pfsense.gateway_groups for failover groups.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/interfaces
actions.summary: List the configured network interfaces — assignment, description, IPv4/IPv6 addressing mode, and MTU. This is the configuration; use interface_status for live link state and counters.
actions.description: List the configured network interfaces — assignment, description, IPv4/IPv6 addressing mode, and MTU. This is the configuration; use interface_status for live link state and counters.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/interfaces
actions.summary: Show per-interface live status — link state, IPv4/IPv6 addresses, media, and in/out packet + byte + error counters. Use to spot a down WAN or errors.
actions.description: Show per-interface live status — link state, IPv4/IPv6 addresses, media, and in/out packet + byte + error counters. Use to spot a down WAN or errors.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/ipsec/child_sas
actions.summary: Show IPsec child SAs (phase 2) — the installed traffic-selector pairs that actually carry tunnel traffic, with state, bytes, and lifetime. Pair with ipsec_status (phase-1 IKE SAs) to tell "tunnel up" from "tunnel passing traffic".
actions.description: Show IPsec child SAs (phase 2) — the installed traffic-selector pairs that actually carry tunnel traffic, with state, bytes, and lifetime. Pair with ipsec_status (phase-1 IKE SAs) to tell "tunnel up" from "tunnel passing traffic".
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/ipsec/sas
actions.summary: Show IPsec security associations — phase-1 IKE SAs and their state (established or not), peers, and algorithms. Use to check whether a site-to-site tunnel is up.
actions.description: Show IPsec security associations — phase-1 IKE SAs and their state (established or not), peers, and algorithms. Use to check whether a site-to-site tunnel is up.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/nat/outbound/mappings
actions.summary: List outbound NAT mappings — how internal traffic is translated leaving the firewall. Use to debug source-NAT and masquerade behaviour.
actions.description: List outbound NAT mappings — how internal traffic is translated leaving the firewall. Use to debug source-NAT and masquerade behaviour.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/nat/port_forwards
actions.summary: List inbound NAT port-forward rules — external port/interface to internal target. Use to audit what is exposed.
actions.description: List inbound NAT port-forward rules — external port/interface to internal target. Use to audit what is exposed.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/logs/openvpn
actions.summary: Show recent OpenVPN log entries — tunnel up/down, client connect/disconnect, and TLS/auth handshake errors. Use to debug VPN connectivity or a client that cannot establish a tunnel. See pfsense.openvpn_status for live session state.
actions.description: Show recent OpenVPN log entries — tunnel up/down, client connect/disconnect, and TLS/auth handshake errors. Use to debug VPN connectivity or a client that cannot establish a tunnel. See pfsense.openvpn_status for live session state.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log lines to return.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/openvpn/servers
actions.summary: Show OpenVPN server status — each server and its connected clients (common name, real address, bytes in/out, connected-since). Use to see who is on the VPN.
actions.description: Show OpenVPN server status — each server and its connected clients (common name, real address, bytes in/out, connected-since). Use to see who is on the VPN.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/diagnostics/table
actions.summary: Show the addresses in one pf table by name (list the names with pfsense.pf_tables) — e.g. which IPs sshguard or a pfBlocker alias is currently blocking. pfBlocker feed tables can hold tens of thousands of entries, so the output is deliberately capped and returned as text; a table larger than the cap is truncated — narrow to a specific smaller table.
actions.description: Show the addresses in one pf table by name (list the names with pfsense.pf_tables) — e.g. which IPs sshguard or a pfBlocker alias is currently blocking. pfBlocker feed tables can hold tens of thousands of entries, so the output is deliberately capped and returned as text; a table larger than the cap is truncated — narrow to a specific smaller table.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: name
args.description: pf table name (from pfsense.pf_tables), e.g. sshguard, bogons, virusprot.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/diagnostics/tables
actions.summary: List the pf table NAMES defined on the firewall (bogons, sshguard, virusprot, pfBlocker aliases, …) — just the names, not their contents. Use pfsense.pf_table to read the addresses in one table (some tables hold tens of thousands of entries).
actions.description: List the pf table NAMES defined on the firewall (bogons, sshguard, virusprot, pfBlocker aliases, …) — just the names, not their contents. Use pfsense.pf_table to read the addresses in one table (some tables hold tens of thousands of entries).
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: POST /api/v2/diagnostics/reboot
actions.summary: Reboot the firewall. All traffic through the box stops until it finishes booting, and there is no remote undo. Use only when a reboot is genuinely required.
actions.description: Reboot the firewall. All traffic through the box stops until it finishes booting, and there is no remote undo. Use only when a reboot is genuinely required.
side_effects: All traffic through the firewall stops until the boot completes.

actions.title: POST /api/v2/status/service (restart)
actions.summary: Restart a pfSense service by name (unbound, dhcpd, openvpn, ipsec, dpinger, …). The service is briefly unavailable while it restarts, so dependent connectivity can blip.
actions.description: Restart a pfSense service by name (unbound, dhcpd, openvpn, ipsec, dpinger, …). The service is briefly unavailable while it restarts, so dependent connectivity can blip.
side_effects: The service is briefly unavailable; dependent connectivity may drop.

args.name: service
args.description: Service name as listed by service_status, e.g. unbound, openvpn, ipsec.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/services
actions.summary: List all pfSense services with their enabled flag and running state (unbound, dhcpd/kea, openvpn, ipsec, dpinger, …). Use to find a stopped service.
actions.description: List all pfSense services with their enabled flag and running state (unbound, dhcpd/kea, openvpn, ipsec, dpinger, …). Use to find a stopped service.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/states/size
actions.summary: Show current firewall state-table count and the configured maximum. Use to watch for state exhaustion. Cheap — does not dump the whole table.
actions.description: Show current firewall state-table count and the configured maximum. Use to watch for state exhaustion. Cheap — does not dump the whole table.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/routing/static_routes
actions.summary: List configured static routes — destination network, gateway, and description. Use to check routing for a reachability problem.
actions.description: List configured static routes — destination network, gateway, and description. Use to check routing for a reachability problem.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/logs/system
actions.summary: Show recent system-log entries — the general pfSense log (boot, services, errors). Use for "what happened on the box recently?".
actions.description: Show recent system-log entries — the general pfSense log (boot, services, errors). Use for "what happened on the box recently?".
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log lines to return.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/system/packages
actions.summary: List the installed pfSense add-on packages with their installed version and whether an update is available. Use to confirm what is loaded on the box (e.g. the FRR or REST API package) and what is out of date.
actions.description: List the installed pfSense add-on packages with their installed version and whether an update is available. Use to confirm what is loaded on the box (e.g. the FRR or REST API package) and what is out of date.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/status/system
actions.summary: Show firewall system health — version, uptime, CPU model/usage/load, memory, swap, mbuf and disk usage, and temperature. The first stop for "how is the box doing?".
actions.description: Show firewall system health — version, uptime, CPU model/usage/load, memory, swap, mbuf and disk usage, and temperature. The first stop for "how is the box doing?".
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/system/version
actions.summary: Show pfSense version, build, and kernel. Cheap connectivity + auth check, and the pack's verify action.
actions.description: Show pfSense version, build, and kernel. Cheap connectivity + auth check, and the pack's verify action.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

actions.title: GET /api/v2/firewall/virtual_ips
actions.summary: List the configured virtual IPs (VIPs) — CARP, IP-alias, and proxy-ARP — with interface, subnet, type, and CARP vhid. Use to audit the shared HA addresses; pair with carp_status for which node currently owns each.
actions.description: List the configured virtual IPs (VIPs) — CARP, IP-alias, and proxy-ARP — with interface, subnet, type, and CARP vhid. Use to audit the shared HA addresses; pair with carp_status for which node currently owns each.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the pfSense REST API.

packs.name: PHP-FPM
packs.description: PHP runtime + FPM pool introspection — version, modules, ini, pool status (via fpm_status endpoint), error + slow logs, OPcache state, Composer manifest. Read-only. Set PHP_FPM_STATUS_URL env to point to the FPM status endpoint.

actions.title: composer show (in $COMPOSER_DIR)
actions.summary: List installed Composer packages with versions.
actions.description: List installed Composer packages with versions.

actions.title: tail FPM error_log
actions.summary: Tail the FPM error log (last N lines).
actions.description: Tail the FPM error log (last N lines).

actions.title: GET /ping (FPM)
actions.summary: Check FPM ping endpoint (returns 'pong' if responsive).
actions.description: Check FPM ping endpoint (returns 'pong' if responsive).

actions.title: GET /status (FPM)
actions.summary: Show FPM pool summary — active processes, idle, max children, listen queue.
actions.description: Show FPM pool summary — active processes, idle, max children, listen queue.

actions.title: GET /status?full (FPM per-process)
actions.summary: Show per-process detail: pid, state, last request URI, current memory peak.
actions.description: Show per-process detail: pid, state, last request URI, current memory peak.

actions.title: php --ini
actions.summary: List all loaded php.ini paths + their contents path.
actions.description: List all loaded php.ini paths + their contents path.

actions.title: php -m
actions.summary: List all loaded extensions.
actions.description: List all loaded extensions.

actions.title: GET /opcache-status
actions.summary: Show OPcache stats (requires the standard opcache_status.php script reachable at PHP_OPCACHE_URL).
actions.description: Show OPcache stats (requires the standard opcache_status.php script reachable at PHP_OPCACHE_URL).

actions.title: tail FPM slow_log
actions.summary: Tail the FPM slow log (last N lines of PHP backtraces for slow requests).
actions.description: Tail the FPM slow log (last N lines of PHP backtraces for slow requests).

actions.title: php -v
actions.summary: Show PHP version + SAPI + Zend Engine version.
actions.description: Show PHP version + SAPI + Zend Engine version.

packs.name: Podman containers
packs.description: Read-only inventory + per-container introspection plus narrow mutators (restart, stop, kill, prune). Drop-in alternative for Docker on RHEL / Fedora hosts. Rootless mode supported as long as the runner uid matches the user that owns the containers.

actions.title: podman images
actions.summary: List all local images.
actions.description: List all local images.

actions.title: podman info
actions.summary: Show system + storage + network info.
actions.description: Show system + storage + network info.

actions.title: podman inspect <id>
actions.summary: Show full container details (state, mounts, network config, env, labels) for one ID/name. Read-only.
actions.description: Show full container details (state, mounts, network config, env, labels) for one ID/name. Read-only.

actions.title: podman kill <id>
actions.summary: Sends SIGKILL: instant termination. In-flight requests are dropped.
actions.description: Sends SIGKILL: instant termination. In-flight requests are dropped.

actions.title: podman logs <id> --tail <N>
actions.summary: Tail container logs (last N lines).
actions.description: Tail container logs (last N lines).

actions.title: podman ps -a
actions.summary: List all containers (running + stopped).
actions.description: List all containers (running + stopped).

actions.title: podman restart <id>
actions.summary: Restart one container. Drops in-flight requests.
actions.description: Restart one container. Drops in-flight requests.
side_effects: Container is stopped (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL) then started.

actions.title: podman stats --no-stream
actions.summary: Show one-shot CPU/mem/net/io stats for all containers.
actions.description: Show one-shot CPU/mem/net/io stats for all containers.

actions.title: podman stop <id>
actions.summary: SIGTERM then SIGKILL after timeout.
actions.description: SIGTERM then SIGKILL after timeout.
side_effects: Restart policy may bring it back unless --rm.

actions.title: podman system df
actions.summary: Show disk usage by images / containers / volumes.
actions.description: Show disk usage by images / containers / volumes.

actions.title: podman system prune -f
actions.summary: Removes stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks.
actions.description: Removes stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks.

actions.title: podman version
actions.summary: Show Podman binary + API + runtime versions.
actions.description: Show Podman binary + API + runtime versions.

packs.name: Postfix mail server
packs.description: Queue inspection, config dump, log tailing, plus narrow operator actions (flush, requeue, delete-by-queue-id). These mutators are high- or critical-risk; the reversible hold/release pause is medium. All are audited. Postfix uid must own the runner OR runner must be in the postdrop group.

actions.title: postfix check
actions.summary: Validates Postfix configuration files.
actions.description: Validates Postfix configuration files.

actions.title: postsuper -d <queue-id>
actions.summary: Deletes one queued message. Permanent — message is lost.
actions.description: Deletes one queued message. Permanent — message is lost.
side_effects: Message is removed from the queue.

actions.title: postqueue -f (retry all deferred)
actions.summary: Forces retry of every deferred message. Can cause a thundering-herd toward downstream SMTP.
actions.description: Forces retry of every deferred message. Can cause a thundering-herd toward downstream SMTP.
side_effects: Every deferred message is re-attempted immediately.
Downstream MTAs see a burst of connection attempts.

actions.title: grep mail.log for queue-id / address
actions.summary: List all mail-log entries matching a string.
actions.description: List all mail-log entries matching a string.

actions.title: tail mail.log
actions.summary: Tail the last N lines of the mail log.
actions.description: Tail the last N lines of the mail log.

actions.title: mailq (current queue)
actions.summary: Show the Postfix mail queue — pending, deferred, hold counts.
actions.description: Show the Postfix mail queue — pending, deferred, hold counts.

actions.title: postcat -q <queue-id>
actions.summary: Decode one queued message — headers + body. Body may contain sensitive content; rely on audit redaction.
actions.description: Decode one queued message — headers + body. Body may contain sensitive content; rely on audit redaction.
side_effects: Read-only — message content may include user PII.

actions.title: postconf -n
actions.summary: Show non-default Postfix config.
actions.description: Show non-default Postfix config.

actions.title: postsuper -h <qid>
actions.summary: Move one message into the hold queue. The message is not delivered until released with postsuper_release. Used when a message needs to be inspected without redelivering on every queue tick.
actions.description: Move one message into the hold queue. The message is not delivered until released with postsuper_release. Used when a message needs to be inspected without redelivering on every queue tick.

actions.title: postsuper -H <qid>
actions.summary: Release one held message back to active. Postfix will attempt delivery on the next queue scan.
actions.description: Release one held message back to active. Postfix will attempt delivery on the next queue scan.
side_effects: Message moved from hold to active.

actions.title: postsuper -r <qid|ALL>
actions.summary: Re-queue one message (or ALL deferred). The message is re-classified from the deferred queue back to active, retrying delivery.
actions.description: Re-queue one message (or ALL deferred). The message is re-classified from the deferred queue back to active, retrying delivery.
side_effects: Message(s) moved from deferred to active.
ALL can cause a delivery burst on a large queue.

actions.title: qshape (queue by domain x age)
actions.summary: Show bucketed queue counts by destination + age.
actions.description: Show bucketed queue counts by destination + age.

actions.title: Per-queue message counts
actions.summary: Count messages in incoming/active/deferred/hold queues.
actions.description: Count messages in incoming/active/deferred/hold queues.

argv: for q in incoming active deferred hold; do n=$(find /var/spool/postfix/$q -type f 2>/dev/null | wc -l); echo "$q: $n"; done

actions.title: postfix reload
actions.summary: Tell Postfix to re-read main.cf and master.cf. Existing deliveries finish on the old config; new ones use the new. No downtime.
actions.description: Tell Postfix to re-read main.cf and master.cf. Existing deliveries finish on the old config; new ones use the new. No downtime.

packs.name: Postgres operations
packs.description: Deep Postgres operations — activity introspection, session/lock diagnostics, table + index analytics (bloat, dead tuples, unused indexes), WAL + replication state, progress views, EXPLAIN, and a curated set of operator-tier mutators (cancel/terminate backend, ANALYZE, VACUUM, REINDEX CONCURRENTLY). Authenticates via PG* env vars on the runner host.

actions.title: pg_stat_activity (per-backend)
actions.summary: Show per-backend detail: pid, user, app, state, wait_event, query age, query text.
actions.description: Show per-backend detail: pid, user, app, state, wait_event, query age, query text.

argv: SELECT pid, usename, application_name, client_addr, state, wait_event_type, wait_event, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-query_start))::int AS query_age_sec, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-state_change))::int AS state_age_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE pid <> pg_backend_pid() ORDER BY query_start ASC NULLS LAST LIMIT 200;

actions.title: Backend counts by state
actions.summary: Show a quick "how many idle / active / idle-in-transaction" health snapshot.
actions.description: Show a quick "how many idle / active / idle-in-transaction" health snapshot.

argv: SELECT state, count(*) AS n FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state ORDER BY n DESC;

actions.title: ANALYZE <schema>.<table>
actions.summary: Refreshes planner statistics for one table.
actions.description: Refreshes planner statistics for one table.
side_effects: Reads sample rows from the table.

actions.title: Backend holding the oldest xmin
actions.summary: Show the backend whose snapshot is preventing vacuum from cleaning dead tuples cluster-wide.
actions.description: Show the backend whose snapshot is preventing vacuum from cleaning dead tuples cluster-wide.

argv: SELECT pid, usename, application_name, state, backend_xmin, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-xact_start))::int AS xact_age_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE backend_xmin IS NOT NULL ORDER BY backend_xmin::text::bigint ASC LIMIT 5;

actions.title: pg_stat_bgwriter
actions.summary: Show background writer + checkpoint stats since stats reset.
actions.description: Show background writer + checkpoint stats since stats reset.

actions.title: Cancel a running query
actions.summary: Sends SIGINT to one backend via `pg_cancel_backend(pid)`. The query aborts but the connection survives. Use to clear a stuck SELECT or a blocker surfaced by `postgres.locks`. If cancel doesn't take effect within seconds the backend is likely stuck in a kernel call — escalate to `kill_idle` (terminate) only as a last resort.
actions.description: Sends SIGINT to one backend via `pg_cancel_backend(pid)`. The query aborts but the connection survives. Use to clear a stuck SELECT or a blocker surfaced by `postgres.locks`. If cancel doesn't take effect within seconds the backend is likely stuck in a kernel call — escalate to `kill_idle` (terminate) only as a last resort.
side_effects: The target backend's current query is cancelled with an ERROR.
The client connection stays open and the transaction is rolled back.

actions.title: Postgres connection summary
actions.summary: Counts pg_stat_activity rows grouped by state and application_name. Surfaces idle-in-transaction backends, connection storms, and per-app traffic skew. Read-only. Pair with `postgres.kill_idle` if the long-idle-in-transaction count is non-zero.
actions.description: Counts pg_stat_activity rows grouped by state and application_name. Surfaces idle-in-transaction backends, connection storms, and per-app traffic skew. Read-only. Pair with `postgres.kill_idle` if the long-idle-in-transaction count is non-zero.

argv: SELECT state, application_name, count(*) AS n FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state, application_name ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 50;

actions.title: pg_stat_database
actions.summary: Show per-DB: commits/rollbacks, blks_read/hit, deadlocks, conflicts, temp file usage.
actions.description: Show per-DB: commits/rollbacks, blks_read/hit, deadlocks, conflicts, temp file usage.

argv: SELECT datname, xact_commit, xact_rollback, blks_read, blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * blks_hit / NULLIF(blks_hit + blks_read, 0), 2) AS cache_hit_pct, tup_returned, tup_fetched, tup_inserted, tup_updated, tup_deleted, deadlocks, conflicts, temp_files, pg_size_pretty(temp_bytes) AS temp_size FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname IS NOT NULL ORDER BY (blks_read + blks_hit) DESC LIMIT 30;

actions.title: All databases by size
actions.summary: List every database with pg_database_size.
actions.description: List every database with pg_database_size.

argv: SELECT datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size FROM pg_database WHERE NOT datistemplate ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;

actions.title: Top tables by dead-tuple ratio
actions.summary: List tables where n_dead_tup / (n_live_tup + n_dead_tup) is high. Bloat suspects.
actions.description: List tables where n_dead_tup / (n_live_tup + n_dead_tup) is high. Bloat suspects.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, ROUND(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_live_tup + n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT 30;

actions.title: Duplicate indexes (same column set)
actions.summary: List indexes covering identical columns. One per group is redundant; drop after verifying.
actions.description: List indexes covering identical columns. One per group is redundant; drop after verifying.

argv: SELECT array_agg(indexrelid::regclass::text ORDER BY indexrelid) AS indexes, indrelid::regclass AS table, indkey::text AS columns FROM pg_index GROUP BY indrelid, indkey HAVING count(*) > 1 LIMIT 50;

actions.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE (FORMAT JSON) <query>
actions.summary: Runs EXPLAIN ANALYZE — the query IS executed (with timing). Forced into a read-only transaction (default_transaction_read_only=on) so data-modifying CTEs and volatile writes are rejected by the server, but the read still runs and can be expensive. High-risk because it executes an arbitrary operator-supplied query on the live database; use explain_query for a plan without execution.
actions.description: Runs EXPLAIN ANALYZE — the query IS executed (with timing). Forced into a read-only transaction (default_transaction_read_only=on) so data-modifying CTEs and volatile writes are rejected by the server, but the read still runs and can be expensive. High-risk because it executes an arbitrary operator-supplied query on the live database; use explain_query for a plan without execution.
side_effects: Query is executed with timing instrumentation, inside a read-only transaction.
Slower than EXPLAIN-only; a heavy query loads the server for up to the timeout.
Data-modifying statements and CTEs (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) are rejected by the read-only transaction.

actions.title: EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON) <query>
actions.summary: Show the plan-only EXPLAIN. The query is NOT executed.
actions.description: Show the plan-only EXPLAIN. The query is NOT executed.
side_effects: Read-only — query body is not executed.

actions.title: Installed extensions
actions.summary: List currently-loaded extensions with versions + schema.
actions.description: List currently-loaded extensions with versions + schema.

argv: SELECT extname, extversion, n.nspname AS schema FROM pg_extension e JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = e.extnamespace ORDER BY extname;

actions.title: HOT update ratio per table
actions.summary: Show HOT update ratio per table. HOT updates avoid index work and bloat. Low ratio on a hot table = missing fillfactor tuning or wrong index.
actions.description: Show HOT update ratio per table. HOT updates avoid index work and bloat. Low ratio on a hot table = missing fillfactor tuning or wrong index.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, n_tup_upd, n_tup_hot_upd, ROUND(100.0 * n_tup_hot_upd / NULLIF(n_tup_upd, 0), 2) AS hot_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_tup_upd > 10000 ORDER BY n_tup_upd DESC LIMIT 30;

actions.title: Idle-in-transaction backends
actions.summary: List backends sitting in 'idle in transaction' state — they hold locks + bloat vacuum's xmin horizon.
actions.description: List backends sitting in 'idle in transaction' state — they hold locks + bloat vacuum's xmin horizon.

argv: SELECT pid, usename, application_name, client_addr, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-state_change))::int AS idle_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS last_query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state IN ('idle in transaction','idle in transaction (aborted)') ORDER BY state_change ASC LIMIT 100;

actions.title: Top 50 indexes by size
actions.summary: List the largest indexes — candidates for bloat investigation.
actions.description: List the largest indexes — candidates for bloat investigation.

argv: SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS table, s.indexrelname AS index, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size, s.idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Invalid indexes (indisvalid = false)
actions.summary: List indexes from failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY — present but not used by the planner.
actions.description: List indexes from failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY — present but not used by the planner.

argv: SELECT n.nspname || '.' || c.relname AS index, t.relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS size FROM pg_index i JOIN pg_class c ON c.oid = i.indexrelid JOIN pg_class t ON t.oid = i.indrelid JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE NOT i.indisvalid;

actions.title: pg_is_in_recovery()
actions.summary: Show whether this instance is a replica (boolean — true if in recovery).
actions.description: Show whether this instance is a replica (boolean — true if in recovery).

actions.title: Terminate idle-in-transaction backends
actions.summary: Calls `pg_terminate_backend(pid)` on every backend that has been in `idle in transaction` state longer than `older_than_seconds`. Frees up the locks they're holding. Application code on the killed connections will see "server closed the connection unexpectedly" and reconnect — any in-flight transaction rolls back. Always check the count via `postgres.connections` first. Do not run during normal traffic.
actions.description: Calls `pg_terminate_backend(pid)` on every backend that has been in `idle in transaction` state longer than `older_than_seconds`. Frees up the locks they're holding. Application code on the killed connections will see "server closed the connection unexpectedly" and reconnect — any in-flight transaction rolls back. Always check the count via `postgres.connections` first. Do not run during normal traffic.
side_effects: In-flight transactions on those connections roll back.
Clients see a closed connection and must reconnect.

args.name: older_than_seconds
args.description: Only terminate idle-in-transaction backends older than this (default 10 min).

argv: SELECT pid, usename, application_name, pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND state_change < now() - interval '{{ args.older_than_seconds }} seconds';

actions.title: Largest tables with vacuum/dead-tuple info
actions.summary: List top 50 tables by size with live + dead tuple counts and last vacuum timestamps.
actions.description: List top 50 tables by size with live + dead tuple counts and last vacuum timestamps.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Last vacuum/analyze per table
actions.summary: List tables ordered by oldest last-vacuum — find ones autovacuum hasn't touched.
actions.description: List tables ordered by oldest last-vacuum — find ones autovacuum hasn't touched.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY COALESCE(last_autovacuum, last_vacuum) ASC NULLS FIRST LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Blocker → blocked chains
actions.summary: List each blocked backend with its blocker. Use to find the head of a stuck lock chain.
actions.description: List each blocked backend with its blocker. Use to find the head of a stuck lock chain.

argv: SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid, blocking.pid AS blocker_pid, blocked.usename AS blocked_user, blocking.usename AS blocker_user, blocked.wait_event_type, blocked.wait_event, substring(blocked.query, 1, 200) AS blocked_query, substring(blocking.query, 1, 200) AS blocker_query FROM pg_stat_activity blocked JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid));

actions.title: Blocking lock graph
actions.summary: Show who's blocking whom. Joins pg_locks with pg_stat_activity to show blocker_pid → blocked_pid pairs plus the truncated SQL of each side. Read-only. Use before a `cancel_query`; you want to cancel the blocker, not the victim.
actions.description: Show who's blocking whom. Joins pg_locks with pg_stat_activity to show blocker_pid → blocked_pid pairs plus the truncated SQL of each side. Read-only. Use before a `cancel_query`; you want to cancel the blocker, not the victim.
side_effects: One SELECT joining pg_locks and pg_stat_activity.
No locks held by this query.

argv: SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid, blocked.usename AS blocked_user, left(blocked.query, 120) AS blocked_query, blocking.pid AS blocking_pid, blocking.usename AS blocking_user, left(blocking.query, 120) AS blocking_query, blocked.wait_event_type, blocked.wait_event FROM pg_stat_activity blocked JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) WHERE blocked.wait_event_type IS NOT NULL ORDER BY blocked.pid LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Top 20 by query age
actions.summary: List backends in 'active' state, oldest first. Use to spot stuck/runaway work.
actions.description: List backends in 'active' state, oldest first. Use to spot stuck/runaway work.

argv: SELECT pid, usename, application_name, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-query_start))::int AS age_sec, wait_event_type, wait_event, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active' AND pid <> pg_backend_pid() ORDER BY query_start ASC NULLS LAST LIMIT 20;

actions.title: pg_hba_file_rules
actions.summary: List effective pg_hba rules as the server loaded them. Catches syntax errors that didn't make it in.
actions.description: List effective pg_hba rules as the server loaded them. Catches syntax errors that didn't make it in.
side_effects: Read-only — LDAP/RADIUS auth secrets are stripped from the options column.

argv: SELECT line_number, type, database, user_name, address, netmask, auth_method, array(SELECT o FROM unnest(options) AS o WHERE o NOT LIKE 'ldapbindpasswd=%' AND o NOT LIKE 'radiussecrets=%') AS options, error FROM pg_hba_file_rules ORDER BY line_number;

actions.title: pg_stat_statements_reset()
actions.summary: Clears accumulated pg_stat_statements counters. Use to start a clean measurement window.
actions.description: Clears accumulated pg_stat_statements counters. Use to start a clean measurement window.
side_effects: All accumulated counters are reset to 0.
Future queries start from a clean baseline.

actions.title: Top statements by total time
actions.summary: Show the top 30 normalized queries by total_exec_time. Requires pg_stat_statements extension to be loaded.
actions.description: Show the top 30 normalized queries by total_exec_time. Requires pg_stat_statements extension to be loaded.

argv: SELECT calls, ROUND(total_exec_time::numeric, 0) AS total_ms, ROUND(mean_exec_time::numeric, 1) AS mean_ms, rows, ROUND(100.0 * shared_blks_hit / NULLIF(shared_blks_hit + shared_blks_read, 0), 1) AS cache_hit_pct, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 30;

actions.title: pg_stat_progress_create_index
actions.summary: Show in-flight CREATE INDEX operations with phase + blocks scanned.
actions.description: Show in-flight CREATE INDEX operations with phase + blocks scanned.

argv: SELECT pid, datname, p.relid::regclass AS table, p.index_relid::regclass AS index, phase, blocks_total, blocks_done, tuples_total, tuples_done FROM pg_stat_progress_create_index p;

actions.title: pg_stat_progress_vacuum
actions.summary: Show in-flight VACUUMs with phase + heap_blks_scanned.
actions.description: Show in-flight VACUUMs with phase + heap_blks_scanned.

argv: SELECT pid, datname, p.relid::regclass AS table, phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned, heap_blks_vacuumed, num_dead_tuples FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum p;

actions.title: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY <schema>.<index>
actions.summary: Rebuilds one index without blocking writes. Slower than plain REINDEX but no AccessExclusiveLock.
actions.description: Rebuilds one index without blocking writes. Slower than plain REINDEX but no AccessExclusiveLock.
side_effects: New copy of the index is built alongside.
Old index dropped at end; brief lock at swap.
On failure, an _ccnew suffix index may be left behind.

actions.title: Reload postgresql.conf
actions.summary: Calls `pg_reload_conf()`. Re-reads the server config without restarting. Picks up changes to settings whose context is `sighup` (logging, autovacuum, work_mem, etc.); does NOT pick up settings marked `postmaster` (shared_buffers, listen_addresses) — those still require a restart. Safe in steady state but considered high-risk because a malformed config can break logging or reset connection limits.
actions.description: Calls `pg_reload_conf()`. Re-reads the server config without restarting. Picks up changes to settings whose context is `sighup` (logging, autovacuum, work_mem, etc.); does NOT pick up settings marked `postmaster` (shared_buffers, listen_addresses) — those still require a restart. Safe in steady state but considered high-risk because a malformed config can break logging or reset connection limits.
side_effects: Active sessions keep their old settings until they reconnect for `user`-context settings; sighup-context settings apply immediately.
A malformed config logs a warning and keeps the previous values.

actions.title: Replication lag (primary view)
actions.summary: Show replication slot health from the primary's perspective. Surfaces sent/write/flush/replay LSNs plus the lag in bytes per replica. Run on the primary. Read-only. A lag >10 MB or a stalled flush_lsn is the usual signal that a downstream replica is in trouble.
actions.description: Show replication slot health from the primary's perspective. Surfaces sent/write/flush/replay LSNs plus the lag in bytes per replica. Run on the primary. Read-only. A lag >10 MB or a stalled flush_lsn is the usual signal that a downstream replica is in trouble.

examples.title: How far behind are my replicas?

argv: SELECT application_name, client_addr, state, sync_state, pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), sent_lsn) AS sent_lag, pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn) AS replay_lag FROM pg_stat_replication ORDER BY application_name;

actions.title: pg_replication_slots
actions.summary: List logical + physical replication slots with retained WAL. Inactive slots that retain WAL forever are a disk-full risk.
actions.description: List logical + physical replication slots with retained WAL. Inactive slots that retain WAL forever are a disk-full risk.

argv: SELECT slot_name, slot_type, plugin, database, active, restart_lsn, confirmed_flush_lsn, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained_wal FROM pg_replication_slots ORDER BY pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) DESC NULLS LAST;

actions.title: Tables with high seq-scan ratio
actions.summary: List tables where seq_scan / (seq_scan + idx_scan) > 50% AND seq_tup_read > 100k. Candidates for missing indexes.
actions.description: List tables where seq_scan / (seq_scan + idx_scan) > 50% AND seq_tup_read > 100k. Candidates for missing indexes.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, seq_scan, idx_scan, seq_tup_read, idx_tup_fetch, ROUND(100.0 * seq_scan / NULLIF(seq_scan + idx_scan, 0), 2) AS seq_scan_pct, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE seq_scan + idx_scan > 0 AND seq_tup_read > 100000 ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC LIMIT 30;

actions.title: pg_settings (non-default)
actions.summary: List settings the operator has changed from the compiled defaults.
actions.description: List settings the operator has changed from the compiled defaults.

argv: SELECT name, setting, unit, source, sourcefile, sourceline FROM pg_settings WHERE source NOT IN ('default', 'override') ORDER BY name;

actions.title: Top slow queries from pg_stat_statements
actions.summary: List the top N query fingerprints by mean execution time from pg_stat_statements. Requires the extension to be loaded (shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'); errors out cleanly if it isn't. Read-only.
actions.description: List the top N query fingerprints by mean execution time from pg_stat_statements. Requires the extension to be loaded (shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'); errors out cleanly if it isn't. Read-only.

args.name: limit
args.description: How many query fingerprints to return.

args.name: min_calls
args.description: Skip query fingerprints with fewer than N total calls (filters one-off DDL noise).

argv: SELECT calls, round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms, round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms, left(query, 200) AS query FROM pg_stat_statements WHERE calls >= {{ args.min_calls }} ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};

actions.title: pg_stat_ssl
actions.summary: List per-backend TLS state: version, cipher, client_serial.
actions.description: List per-backend TLS state: version, cipher, client_serial.

argv: SELECT s.pid, a.usename, a.application_name, s.ssl, s.version, s.cipher FROM pg_stat_ssl s JOIN pg_stat_activity a USING (pid) ORDER BY s.ssl DESC, s.pid LIMIT 100;

actions.title: pg_statio_user_tables
actions.summary: Show per-table heap + index buffer reads vs hits. Bad cache hit rate? Find the table.
actions.description: Show per-table heap + index buffer reads vs hits. Bad cache hit rate? Find the table.

argv: SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, heap_blks_read, heap_blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * heap_blks_hit / NULLIF(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read, 0), 2) AS heap_hit_pct, idx_blks_read, idx_blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * idx_blks_hit / NULLIF(idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read, 0), 2) AS idx_hit_pct FROM pg_statio_user_tables ORDER BY heap_blks_read + idx_blks_read DESC LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Top tables by total size
actions.summary: List the top N tables by total size (heap + indexes + toast) for one schema. Use to find the table that's dominating disk before recommending vacuum, archive, or partitioning. Read-only.
actions.description: List the top N tables by total size (heap + indexes + toast) for one schema. Use to find the table that's dominating disk before recommending vacuum, archive, or partitioning. Read-only.

argv: SELECT n.nspname AS schema, c.relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(c.oid)) AS total, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS heap, pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(c.oid)) AS indexes FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE c.relkind = 'r' AND n.nspname = '{{ args.schema }}' ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(c.oid) DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};

actions.title: pg_terminate_backend(pid)
actions.summary: Hard-disconnects one backend (SIGTERM). Use when pg_cancel_backend isn't enough (e.g., idle in transaction with a long held lock).
actions.description: Hard-disconnects one backend (SIGTERM). Use when pg_cancel_backend isn't enough (e.g., idle in transaction with a long held lock).

actions.title: Unused indexes (idx_scan = 0)
actions.summary: List indexes never used since last stats reset. Drop candidates — but verify they're not for an unrelated path (e.g., uniqueness constraint).
actions.description: List indexes never used since last stats reset. Drop candidates — but verify they're not for an unrelated path (e.g., uniqueness constraint).

argv: SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS table, s.indexrelname AS index, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size, i.indisunique, i.indisprimary FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid WHERE s.idx_scan = 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 50;

actions.title: Postgres uptime and version
actions.summary: Show server uptime, version, and current connection count. Reads pg_stat_database + pg_postmaster_start_time(). Use as a first-touch sanity check before deeper diagnosis. Read-only.
actions.description: Show server uptime, version, and current connection count. Reads pg_stat_database + pg_postmaster_start_time(). Use as a first-touch sanity check before deeper diagnosis. Read-only.
side_effects: Issues two SELECTs against system catalogs.

argv: SELECT version(); SELECT pg_postmaster_start_time(); SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;

actions.title: Autovacuum + bloat snapshot
actions.summary: Show last-vacuum/last-autovacuum timestamps and dead-tuple counts for the top N tables in one schema, ordered by dead tuples. Use to decide whether to run VACUUM manually or tune autovacuum. Read-only.
actions.description: Show last-vacuum/last-autovacuum timestamps and dead-tuple counts for the top N tables in one schema, ordered by dead tuples. Use to decide whether to run VACUUM manually or tune autovacuum. Read-only.

examples.title: Find tables with the most dead rows

argv: SELECT schemaname, relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, round(100 * n_dead_tup::numeric / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE schemaname = '{{ args.schema }}' ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};

actions.title: VACUUM <schema>.<table>
actions.summary: Reclaims dead-tuple space in one table. Non-blocking (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock). Use VACUUM ANALYZE if planner stats are also stale.
actions.description: Reclaims dead-tuple space in one table. Non-blocking (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock). Use VACUUM ANALYZE if planner stats are also stale.
side_effects: Does NOT shrink the table file — for that, use VACUUM FULL (not exposed here; rebuilds the table with AccessExclusiveLock).

actions.title: pg_stat_archiver
actions.summary: Show WAL archiver stats: archived/failed counts, last archived WAL, last failure.
actions.description: Show WAL archiver stats: archived/failed counts, last archived WAL, last failure.

actions.title: Current WAL LSN + recovery state
actions.summary: Show a snapshot of current WAL LSN, last receive/replay LSNs, recovery state.
actions.description: Show a snapshot of current WAL LSN, last receive/replay LSNs, recovery state.

argv: SELECT pg_is_in_recovery() AS in_recovery, CASE WHEN pg_is_in_recovery() THEN NULL ELSE pg_current_wal_lsn() END AS current_lsn, pg_last_wal_receive_lsn() AS last_receive_lsn, pg_last_wal_replay_lsn() AS last_replay_lsn, pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS last_replay_time;

actions.title: How close are we to XID wraparound?
actions.summary: Show per-database age(datfrozenxid). 2^31 (~2.1B) is the wraparound limit. >1B = pay attention; >1.8B = emergency.
actions.description: Show per-database age(datfrozenxid). 2^31 (~2.1B) is the wraparound limit. >1B = pay attention; >1.8B = emergency.

argv: SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age, ROUND(100.0 * age(datfrozenxid) / 2147483648.0, 2) AS pct_to_wraparound FROM pg_database ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;

SELECT pid, usename, application_name, client_addr, state, wait_event_type, wait_event, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-query_start))::int AS query_age_sec, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-state_change))::int AS state_age_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE pid <> pg_backend_pid() ORDER BY query_start ASC NULLS LAST LIMIT 200;
SELECT state, count(*) AS n FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state ORDER BY n DESC;

SELECT pid, usename, application_name, state, backend_xmin, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-xact_start))::int AS xact_age_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE backend_xmin IS NOT NULL ORDER BY backend_xmin::text::bigint ASC LIMIT 5;
SELECT state, application_name, count(*) AS n FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state, application_name ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 50;

SELECT datname, xact_commit, xact_rollback, blks_read, blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * blks_hit / NULLIF(blks_hit + blks_read, 0), 2) AS cache_hit_pct, tup_returned, tup_fetched, tup_inserted, tup_updated, tup_deleted, deadlocks, conflicts, temp_files, pg_size_pretty(temp_bytes) AS temp_size FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname IS NOT NULL ORDER BY (blks_read + blks_hit) DESC LIMIT 30;
SELECT datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size FROM pg_database WHERE NOT datistemplate ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, ROUND(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_live_tup + n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT 30;
SELECT array_agg(indexrelid::regclass::text ORDER BY indexrelid) AS indexes, indrelid::regclass AS table, indkey::text AS columns FROM pg_index GROUP BY indrelid, indkey HAVING count(*) > 1 LIMIT 50;
SELECT extname, extversion, n.nspname AS schema FROM pg_extension e JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = e.extnamespace ORDER BY extname;

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, n_tup_upd, n_tup_hot_upd, ROUND(100.0 * n_tup_hot_upd / NULLIF(n_tup_upd, 0), 2) AS hot_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_tup_upd > 10000 ORDER BY n_tup_upd DESC LIMIT 30;

SELECT pid, usename, application_name, client_addr, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-state_change))::int AS idle_sec, substring(query, 1, 300) AS last_query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state IN ('idle in transaction','idle in transaction (aborted)') ORDER BY state_change ASC LIMIT 100;

SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS table, s.indexrelname AS index, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size, s.idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 50;

SELECT n.nspname || '.' || c.relname AS index, t.relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS size FROM pg_index i JOIN pg_class c ON c.oid = i.indexrelid JOIN pg_class t ON t.oid = i.indrelid JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE NOT i.indisvalid;
SELECT pid, usename, application_name, pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND state_change < now() - interval '{{ args.older_than_seconds }} seconds';

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC LIMIT 50;

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY COALESCE(last_autovacuum, last_vacuum) ASC NULLS FIRST LIMIT 50;

SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid, blocking.pid AS blocker_pid, blocked.usename AS blocked_user, blocking.usename AS blocker_user, blocked.wait_event_type, blocked.wait_event, substring(blocked.query, 1, 200) AS blocked_query, substring(blocking.query, 1, 200) AS blocker_query FROM pg_stat_activity blocked JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid));

SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid, blocked.usename AS blocked_user, left(blocked.query, 120) AS blocked_query, blocking.pid AS blocking_pid, blocking.usename AS blocking_user, left(blocking.query, 120) AS blocking_query, blocked.wait_event_type, blocked.wait_event FROM pg_stat_activity blocked JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) WHERE blocked.wait_event_type IS NOT NULL ORDER BY blocked.pid LIMIT 50;

SELECT pid, usename, application_name, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now()-query_start))::int AS age_sec, wait_event_type, wait_event, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active' AND pid <> pg_backend_pid() ORDER BY query_start ASC NULLS LAST LIMIT 20;

SELECT line_number, type, database, user_name, address, netmask, auth_method, array(SELECT o FROM unnest(options) AS o WHERE o NOT LIKE 'ldapbindpasswd=%' AND o NOT LIKE 'radiussecrets=%') AS options, error FROM pg_hba_file_rules ORDER BY line_number;

SELECT calls, ROUND(total_exec_time::numeric, 0) AS total_ms, ROUND(mean_exec_time::numeric, 1) AS mean_ms, rows, ROUND(100.0 * shared_blks_hit / NULLIF(shared_blks_hit + shared_blks_read, 0), 1) AS cache_hit_pct, substring(query, 1, 300) AS query FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 30;
SELECT pid, datname, p.relid::regclass AS table, p.index_relid::regclass AS index, phase, blocks_total, blocks_done, tuples_total, tuples_done FROM pg_stat_progress_create_index p;
SELECT pid, datname, p.relid::regclass AS table, phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned, heap_blks_vacuumed, num_dead_tuples FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum p;

SELECT application_name, client_addr, state, sync_state, pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), sent_lsn) AS sent_lag, pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn) AS replay_lag FROM pg_stat_replication ORDER BY application_name;

SELECT slot_name, slot_type, plugin, database, active, restart_lsn, confirmed_flush_lsn, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained_wal FROM pg_replication_slots ORDER BY pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) DESC NULLS LAST;

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, seq_scan, idx_scan, seq_tup_read, idx_tup_fetch, ROUND(100.0 * seq_scan / NULLIF(seq_scan + idx_scan, 0), 2) AS seq_scan_pct, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE seq_scan + idx_scan > 0 AND seq_tup_read > 100000 ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC LIMIT 30;
SELECT name, setting, unit, source, sourcefile, sourceline FROM pg_settings WHERE source NOT IN ('default', 'override') ORDER BY name;

SELECT calls, round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms, round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms, left(query, 200) AS query FROM pg_stat_statements WHERE calls >= {{ args.min_calls }} ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};
SELECT s.pid, a.usename, a.application_name, s.ssl, s.version, s.cipher FROM pg_stat_ssl s JOIN pg_stat_activity a USING (pid) ORDER BY s.ssl DESC, s.pid LIMIT 100;

SELECT schemaname || '.' || relname AS table, heap_blks_read, heap_blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * heap_blks_hit / NULLIF(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read, 0), 2) AS heap_hit_pct, idx_blks_read, idx_blks_hit, ROUND(100.0 * idx_blks_hit / NULLIF(idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read, 0), 2) AS idx_hit_pct FROM pg_statio_user_tables ORDER BY heap_blks_read + idx_blks_read DESC LIMIT 50;

SELECT n.nspname AS schema, c.relname AS table, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(c.oid)) AS total, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS heap, pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(c.oid)) AS indexes FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE c.relkind = 'r' AND n.nspname = '{{ args.schema }}' ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(c.oid) DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};

SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS table, s.indexrelname AS index, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size, i.indisunique, i.indisprimary FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid WHERE s.idx_scan = 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 50;
SELECT version(); SELECT pg_postmaster_start_time(); SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;

SELECT schemaname, relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, round(100 * n_dead_tup::numeric / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE schemaname = '{{ args.schema }}' ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT {{ args.limit }};

SELECT pg_is_in_recovery() AS in_recovery, CASE WHEN pg_is_in_recovery() THEN NULL ELSE pg_current_wal_lsn() END AS current_lsn, pg_last_wal_receive_lsn() AS last_receive_lsn, pg_last_wal_replay_lsn() AS last_replay_lsn, pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS last_replay_time;

actions.title: How close are we to XID wraparound?

argv: SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age, ROUND(100.0 * age(datfrozenxid) / 2147483648.0, 2) AS pct_to_wraparound FROM pg_database ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;

packs.name: Process forensics
packs.description: Deep per-process diagnostics for "why is this process stuck / slow / leaking?" — strace (HIGH RISK; slows the target), pid memory maps, per-thread state, /proc walking, gdb backtrace, full lsof, syscall summary. Read-only — but strace and gdb attach via ptrace and WILL slow the target.

actions.title: gdb -ex "thread apply all bt"
actions.summary: Attaches gdb to one PID and dumps native stack for every thread, then detaches. The target is briefly stopped during the dump.
actions.description: Attaches gdb to one PID and dumps native stack for every thread, then detaches. The target is briefly stopped during the dump.
side_effects: Target process is paused for the duration of the gdb session (typically 0.5-3 seconds).

argv: gdb -p {{ args.pid }} -batch -ex 'set pagination off' -ex 'thread apply all bt' -ex 'detach' -ex 'quit' 2>&1 | head -5000

actions.title: /proc/PID/io
actions.summary: Show cumulative read/write byte counts for one PID. Use to identify IO hogs.
actions.description: Show cumulative read/write byte counts for one PID. Use to identify IO hogs.

actions.title: /proc/PID/maps
actions.summary: Show memory map for one PID. Use to see loaded libraries + heap/stack ranges.
actions.description: Show memory map for one PID. Use to see loaded libraries + heap/stack ranges.

actions.title: lsof -p <pid>
actions.summary: List all open files (regular, sockets, pipes) for one PID. Richer than /proc/PID/fd alone.
actions.description: List all open files (regular, sockets, pipes) for one PID. Richer than /proc/PID/fd alone.

actions.title: /proc/PID/smaps_rollup
actions.summary: Show aggregated memory stats — Rss, Pss, Shared, Private. Better than RSS for diagnosing memory pressure.
