actions.description: Inspect a PKCS#12 bundle's bag contents — cert subjects, key types — without exporting the private key. Requires the bundle's password via PK12_PASSWORD env var on the runner host (or empty for password-less bundles).

actions.title: openssl verify -CAfile <bundle> <cert>
actions.summary: Verify that a cert chains to a trusted root via a given CA bundle.
actions.description: Verify that a cert chains to a trusted root via a given CA bundle.

packs.name: Systemd deep introspection pack
packs.description: Deeper systemd state than linux-core: failed units, list-units, timers, cgroup tree, journal disk usage, systemd-analyze for boot diagnosis, plus full operator surface (daemon-reload, start/stop/ restart/reload, kill -s signal, mask/unmask, reset-failed) for remediating runtime issues.

actions.title: systemd-analyze blame
actions.summary: Show the top 30 units sorted by how long they took to start. Use to find a slow boot.
actions.description: Show the top 30 units sorted by how long they took to start. Use to find a slow boot.

actions.title: systemd-analyze critical-chain
actions.summary: Shows the boot-time critical path leading to one unit — useful for understanding "why did multi-user.target take so long?"
actions.description: Shows the boot-time critical path leading to one unit — useful for understanding "why did multi-user.target take so long?"

actions.title: systemd-analyze security
actions.summary: Scores one unit by its sandboxing posture — NoNewPrivileges, ProtectSystem, CapabilityBoundingSet, etc. Use to audit "how locked down is this service?"
actions.description: Scores one unit by its sandboxing posture — NoNewPrivileges, ProtectSystem, CapabilityBoundingSet, etc. Use to audit "how locked down is this service?"

actions.title: Boot-time journal errors
actions.summary: `journalctl -b -p err` — every error-priority log line from the current boot. Use to triage a "is everything healthy?" check.
actions.description: `journalctl -b -p err` — every error-priority log line from the current boot. Use to triage a "is everything healthy?" check.

actions.title: systemd-cgtop (3 samples)
actions.summary: Show three batched cgtop samples — CPU + memory + IO + tasks per cgroup. The systemd-aware top equivalent.
actions.description: Show three batched cgtop samples — CPU + memory + IO + tasks per cgroup. The systemd-aware top equivalent.

actions.title: systemd cgroup tree
actions.summary: `systemd-cgls` — the full cgroup hierarchy with process names. Use to understand resource accounting.
actions.description: `systemd-cgls` — the full cgroup hierarchy with process names. Use to understand resource accounting.

actions.title: systemctl daemon-reload
actions.summary: Re-read unit files from disk. Required after editing a unit file or installing a new package. Without this, systemctl restart still uses the old unit definition.
actions.description: Re-read unit files from disk. Required after editing a unit file or installing a new package. Without this, systemctl restart still uses the old unit definition.
side_effects: Running units unaffected; their definitions update on next restart.

actions.title: Failed systemd units
actions.summary: `systemctl --failed` — every unit not in `active` state with its last failure reason. The fast "what is broken on this host?" check.
actions.description: `systemctl --failed` — every unit not in `active` state with its last failure reason. The fast "what is broken on this host?" check.

actions.title: journalctl --disk-usage
actions.summary: Show how much disk the systemd journal is using.
actions.description: Show how much disk the systemd journal is using.

actions.title: List installed unit files
actions.summary: `systemctl list-unit-files --type=service` — every service file on disk with its enabled/disabled state.
actions.description: `systemctl list-unit-files --type=service` — every service file on disk with its enabled/disabled state.

actions.title: List all systemd units
actions.summary: `systemctl list-units --type=service --all` — every service known to systemd, active or inactive.
actions.description: `systemctl list-units --type=service --all` — every service known to systemd, active or inactive.

actions.title: systemctl reset-failed [unit]
actions.summary: Clear the "failed" state from units that crashed. Required before start-limit thresholds reset and the unit can be auto-restarted. Without a unit name, resets every failed unit.
actions.description: Clear the "failed" state from units that crashed. Required before start-limit thresholds reset and the unit can be auto-restarted. Without a unit name, resets every failed unit.
side_effects: Unit not started — explicit start still needed if desired.

args.name: unit
args.description: Unit name (empty for all failed units).

actions.title: systemd-managed sockets
actions.summary: `systemctl list-sockets` — every socket-activated unit with its listening address.
actions.description: `systemctl list-sockets` — every socket-activated unit with its listening address.

actions.title: Active systemd timers
actions.summary: `systemctl list-timers` — every timer with its next-elapse and last-trigger. The systemd replacement for crontab.
actions.description: `systemctl list-timers` — every timer with its next-elapse and last-trigger. The systemd replacement for crontab.

actions.title: systemctl kill -s <signal> <unit>
actions.summary: Send a signal to a unit's main process (or all processes). Useful when a service is wedged and SIGTERM-via-stop isn't working. SIGKILL is unrecoverable mid-syscall; consider SIGTERM/SIGHUP first.
actions.description: Send a signal to a unit's main process (or all processes). Useful when a service is wedged and SIGTERM-via-stop isn't working. SIGKILL is unrecoverable mid-syscall; consider SIGTERM/SIGHUP first.
side_effects: Behavior depends on signal — SIGHUP often triggers config reload.

actions.title: systemctl mask <unit>
actions.summary: Prevent a unit from being started, even by dependencies. Symlinks the unit to /dev/null. Used to disable a unit that another package keeps re-enabling. Reversible with unmask.
actions.description: Prevent a unit from being started, even by dependencies. Symlinks the unit to /dev/null. Used to disable a unit that another package keeps re-enabling. Reversible with unmask.
side_effects: Unit cannot be started until unmasked.
Dependency chains referencing this unit may fail to activate.

actions.title: systemctl reload <unit>
actions.summary: Ask one unit to reload its config without restarting. Only works if the unit has ExecReload= defined. Use over `restart` whenever possible — no downtime.
actions.description: Ask one unit to reload its config without restarting. Only works if the unit has ExecReload= defined. Use over `restart` whenever possible — no downtime.

actions.title: systemctl restart <unit>
actions.summary: Stop then start one unit. The service is unavailable during the gap (typically <1s for healthy services, much longer if it has a long shutdown). Workload-bearing units mean a real outage — prefer reload when supported.
actions.description: Stop then start one unit. The service is unavailable during the gap (typically <1s for healthy services, much longer if it has a long shutdown). Workload-bearing units mean a real outage — prefer reload when supported.
side_effects: PID changes; any reverse-proxy or supervisor must re-resolve.

actions.title: systemctl show <unit>
actions.summary: Show the full property dump for one unit — every directive (CPUShares, MemoryMax, Restart, ExecStart, etc). This surfaces the unit's `Environment=` values and full ExecStart command line, which commonly carry injected secrets (DB URLs, API keys, tokens). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
actions.description: Show the full property dump for one unit — every directive (CPUShares, MemoryMax, Restart, ExecStart, etc). This surfaces the unit's `Environment=` values and full ExecStart command line, which commonly carry injected secrets (DB URLs, API keys, tokens). The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but exposes the unit's Environment= values and ExecStart (may include secrets).

actions.title: systemctl start <unit>
actions.summary: Start one unit. If already running, no-op. Use after a stop or reset_failed to bring a service back.
actions.description: Start one unit. If already running, no-op. Use after a stop or reset_failed to bring a service back.

actions.title: systemctl stop <unit>
actions.summary: Stop one unit. Workload-bearing units stay down until manually started or auto-restarted by `Restart=`. Use during incident containment or planned downtime.
actions.description: Stop one unit. Workload-bearing units stay down until manually started or auto-restarted by `Restart=`. Use during incident containment or planned downtime.
side_effects: Stays stopped until started again (or Restart= triggers).

actions.title: systemctl unmask <unit>
actions.summary: Undo `mask` for one unit. The unit can be started again.
actions.description: Undo `mask` for one unit. The unit can be started again.
side_effects: Unit not started — explicit start still needed.

actions.title: Vacuum the systemd journal
actions.summary: Drops journal records older than the cutoff time. Frees disk but loses forensic history. Provide cutoff as a journalctl --vacuum-time value (e.g. "7d", "30d").
actions.description: Drops journal records older than the cutoff time. Frees disk but loses forensic history. Provide cutoff as a journalctl --vacuum-time value (e.g. "7d", "30d").
side_effects: Deletes journal records older than the cutoff.

actions.title: Watchdog status across units
actions.summary: Lists every unit with a non-zero WatchdogUSec property — services that have systemd-level watchdog supervision.
actions.description: Lists every unit with a non-zero WatchdogUSec property — services that have systemd-level watchdog supervision.
side_effects: Iterates systemctl show across all units.

argv: systemctl list-units --type=service --plain --no-legend --no-pager | awk '{print $1}' | while read u; do v=$(systemctl show "$u" -p WatchdogUSec --value 2>/dev/null); if [ -n "$v" ] && [ "$v" != "0" ]; then echo "$u: $v"; fi; done

packs.name: Tailscale
packs.description: Inspect the host's Tailscale node via the tailscale CLI: tailnet status and peer/online map, network connectivity (netcheck DERP latency, port-mapping), ping to peers, whois lookups, this node's tailnet IPs, version, available exit nodes, MagicDNS/DNS config, and the current prefs (advertised routes, configured exit node). Read-only.

actions.title: tailscale debug prefs
actions.summary: Show current node prefs as JSON, including AdvertiseRoutes (this node's advertised subnet routes) and ExitNodeIP/ExitNodeID (the configured exit node, if any). Read-only print of the in-memory prefs.
actions.description: Show current node prefs as JSON, including AdvertiseRoutes (this node's advertised subnet routes) and ExitNodeIP/ExitNodeID (the configured exit node, if any). Read-only print of the in-memory prefs.

examples.title: Current prefs (advertised routes, configured exit node)

actions.title: tailscale dns status
actions.summary: Show MagicDNS and DNS configuration for this node — nameservers, search domains, split-DNS routes, and whether MagicDNS is enabled.
actions.description: Show MagicDNS and DNS configuration for this node — nameservers, search domains, split-DNS routes, and whether MagicDNS is enabled.

actions.title: tailscale exit-node list
actions.summary: List available exit nodes on the tailnet (hostname, country, city, status). Succeeds with no rows when the tailnet has no exit nodes (a benign empty state); a real failure (tailscaled down / not logged in) still reports failed.
actions.description: List available exit nodes on the tailnet (hostname, country, city, status). Succeeds with no rows when the tailnet has no exit nodes (a benign empty state); a real failure (tailscaled down / not logged in) still reports failed.

argv:
out=$(tailscale exit-node list 2>&1); rc=$?
printf '%s\n' "$out"
if [ "$rc" -eq 0 ] || printf '%s' "$out" | grep -q 'no exit nodes found'; then exit 0; fi
exit "$rc"

actions.title: tailscale ip
actions.summary: Show this node's tailnet IPs (IPv4 and IPv6).
actions.description: Show this node's tailnet IPs (IPv4 and IPv6).

actions.title: tailscale netcheck --format=json
actions.summary: Show network connectivity report as JSON — DERP latency, UDP reachability, IPv4/IPv6 support, port-mapping (UPnP/PMP/PCP), and PreferredDERP.
actions.description: Show network connectivity report as JSON — DERP latency, UDP reachability, IPv4/IPv6 support, port-mapping (UPnP/PMP/PCP), and PreferredDERP.

actions.title: tailscale ping <host>
actions.summary: Ping a tailnet peer over Tailscale and report the path (direct vs DERP) and latency. Terminates on its own — the bounded count (default 5, max 20) means it stops after that many replies or attempts.
actions.description: Ping a tailnet peer over Tailscale and report the path (direct vs DERP) and latency. Terminates on its own — the bounded count (default 5, max 20) means it stops after that many replies or attempts.
side_effects: Sends Tailscale ping probes to one peer.

args.name: host
args.description: Peer to ping — a tailnet hostname, MagicDNS name, or Tailscale IP.

examples.title: Ping a peer by MagicDNS name

actions.title: tailscale status --json
actions.summary: Show tailnet status as JSON — the peer/online map: Self, Peer{}, Online, ExitNodeStatus, TailscaleIPs, and Health.
actions.description: Show tailnet status as JSON — the peer/online map: Self, Peer{}, Online, ExitNodeStatus, TailscaleIPs, and Health.

actions.title: tailscale version --json
actions.summary: Show Tailscale client version and build info as JSON.
actions.description: Show Tailscale client version and build info as JSON.

actions.title: tailscale whois --json <ip>
actions.summary: Identify which tailnet node and user own a Tailscale IP — returns the node, user, and capabilities as JSON.
actions.description: Identify which tailnet node and user own a Tailscale IP — returns the node, user, and capabilities as JSON.

args.name: ip
args.description: Tailscale IP (IPv4 or IPv6) to look up.

packs.name: Terraform read-only ops
packs.description: Read-only Terraform CLI actions for inspecting a workspace. NO apply, NO destroy, NO state mutation — those should happen in CI, not via a runner. Operates in the directory given by TF_DIR env var.

actions.title: terraform output -json
actions.summary: Show the workspace's output values (terraform output -json). The -json form deliberately un-masks outputs marked `sensitive`, so the values routinely carry secrets (a db_password, a kubeconfig, provider credentials) in cleartext; scope it by policy. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can't reliably mask output values whose arbitrary names and shapes match no rule.
actions.description: Show the workspace's output values (terraform output -json). The -json form deliberately un-masks outputs marked `sensitive`, so the values routinely carry secrets (a db_password, a kubeconfig, provider credentials) in cleartext; scope it by policy. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can't reliably mask output values whose arbitrary names and shapes match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but un-masks `sensitive` outputs (may include secrets).

actions.title: terraform plan
actions.summary: Computes a plan but does NOT save it (no -out). Cannot be applied from this run. May call out to providers (read API quotas).
actions.description: Computes a plan but does NOT save it (no -out). Cannot be applied from this run. May call out to providers (read API quotas).
side_effects: Calls every provider's read API — quota usage applies.
Does not modify state or save a plan file.

actions.title: terraform providers
actions.summary: List all providers declared in the workspace + their constraints.
actions.description: List all providers declared in the workspace + their constraints.

actions.title: terraform show -json
actions.summary: Show the full workspace state as JSON (terraform show -json), for programmatic queries. This dumps every resource's attributes, including ones marked `sensitive` (passwords, private keys, tokens), in cleartext; scope it by policy. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can't reliably mask secrets whose names and shapes match no rule.
actions.description: Show the full workspace state as JSON (terraform show -json), for programmatic queries. This dumps every resource's attributes, including ones marked `sensitive` (passwords, private keys, tokens), in cleartext; scope it by policy. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can't reliably mask secrets whose names and shapes match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but dumps every resource attribute (may include secrets).

actions.title: terraform state list
actions.summary: List all resource addresses currently in state.
actions.description: List all resource addresses currently in state.

actions.title: terraform state show <address>
actions.summary: Show attributes of one resource in state. May contain secrets — relies on audit redaction.
actions.description: Show attributes of one resource in state. May contain secrets — relies on audit redaction.
side_effects: Read-only — output may contain sensitive attributes; rely on redaction.

actions.title: terraform validate
actions.summary: Validates the workspace's HCL files. No state read or write.
actions.description: Validates the workspace's HCL files. No state read or write.

actions.title: terraform version
actions.summary: Show the Terraform binary version + provider versions.
actions.description: Show the Terraform binary version + provider versions.

packs.name: Time sync diagnostics
packs.description: Clock-sync state: chrony tracking + sources, NTP peer status, current time, drift estimate, timezone, plus fix actions (chronyc makestep, enable/disable NTP). Use when a host's clock is drifting (a common cause of TLS / auth / log-correlation bugs).

actions.title: chronyc sources -v
actions.summary: List NTP sources + reach + stratum + offset.
actions.description: List NTP sources + reach + stratum + offset.

actions.title: chronyc tracking
actions.summary: Show Chrony's view of clock drift, frequency, last update.
actions.description: Show Chrony's view of clock drift, frequency, last update.

actions.title: chronyc makestep
actions.summary: Force chrony to step the system clock NOW instead of slewing. Use when drift exceeds slew tolerance and applications can't wait. Jumps in time can confuse cert validation, log timestamps, and event ordering.
actions.description: Force chrony to step the system clock NOW instead of slewing. Use when drift exceeds slew tolerance and applications can't wait. Jumps in time can confuse cert validation, log timestamps, and event ordering.
side_effects: System clock jumps to NTP-synced time.
Cron jobs in the skipped window do NOT fire.
TLS cert validation may briefly fail mid-step.

actions.title: date -u
actions.summary: Show UTC + local time + day-of-week. Compare against the requester's clock to spot drift.
actions.description: Show UTC + local time + day-of-week. Compare against the requester's clock to spot drift.

actions.title: ntpq -pn
actions.summary: List the NTP peer table (for systems still using ntpd rather than chrony).
actions.description: List the NTP peer table (for systems still using ntpd rather than chrony).

actions.title: timedatectl status
actions.summary: Show current time, timezone, NTP-active state, RTC sync state.
actions.description: Show current time, timezone, NTP-active state, RTC sync state.

actions.title: timedatectl set-ntp <bool>
actions.summary: Enable or disable NTP synchronization. Disabling is rare — primarily for offline testing.
actions.description: Enable or disable NTP synchronization. Disabling is rare — primarily for offline testing.
side_effects: When disabled, the clock will drift.

packs.name: Traefik ingress / reverse proxy
packs.description: Read-only visibility into a Traefik (v2/v3) edge router over its HTTP API: the overview, entrypoints, and the full HTTP/TCP/UDP router + service + middleware inventory (each carrying its status and error list, so you can see which router is broken), a compact per-service health summary, plus a per-host readiness check that joins the router and service views into one cutover-preflight verdict, the raw dynamic-config dump, version, liveness ping, and Prometheus metrics. ACME/Let's Encrypt certificate state is read from the on-disk acme.json (no API exposes it), and access-log 4xx/5xx tails mirror the nginx pack. Default API at http://127.0.0.1:8080 (api.insecure mode); override via TRAEFIK_URL.

actions.title: ACME certificate expiry (from acme.json)
actions.summary: Show each ACME/Let's Encrypt certificate in acme.json with its domain and notAfter expiry date. Decodes only the public certificate (base64 PEM) through openssl — never touches the private key. Traefik auto-renews 30 days out, so anything closer than that which is NOT renewing is the thing to investigate. Read-only.
actions.description: Show each ACME/Let's Encrypt certificate in acme.json with its domain and notAfter expiry date. Decodes only the public certificate (base64 PEM) through openssl — never touches the private key. Traefik auto-renews 30 days out, so anything closer than that which is NOT renewing is the thing to investigate. Read-only.
side_effects: Reads the acme.json storage file (public certificate fields only).

args.name: acme_path
args.description: Path to Traefik's ACME storage file.

argv: jq -r 'to_entries[].value.Certificates[]? | "\(.domain.main) \(.certificate)"' {{ args.acme_path }} | while read -r dom cert; do exp=$(printf '%s' "$cert" | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate 2>/dev/null | cut -d= -f2); printf '%s\texpires %s\n' "$dom" "${exp:-<unreadable>}"; done

actions.title: ACME certificate domains (from acme.json)
actions.summary: Lists the domains (CN + SANs) Traefik holds ACME/Let's Encrypt certificates for, read from the on-disk acme.json storage (no API exposes cert state). Reads only the domain fields — never the certificate or private-key material. Use to confirm a hostname actually has an issued cert.
actions.description: Lists the domains (CN + SANs) Traefik holds ACME/Let's Encrypt certificates for, read from the on-disk acme.json storage (no API exposes cert state). Reads only the domain fields — never the certificate or private-key material. Use to confirm a hostname actually has an issued cert.
side_effects: Reads the acme.json storage file (domain fields only).

args.name: acme_path
args.description: Path to Traefik's ACME storage file. Common locations are /etc/traefik/acme.json or /letsencrypt/acme.json.

actions.title: GET /api/entrypoints
actions.summary: List all configured entrypoints — name, listen address/port, and transport settings (timeouts, HTTP/2, TLS defaults). Use to confirm the front door is listening where you expect (web :80, websecure :443, etc.).
actions.description: List all configured entrypoints — name, listen address/port, and transport settings (timeouts, HTTP/2, TLS defaults). Use to confirm the front door is listening where you expect (web :80, websecure :443, etc.).
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: Check whether a public host is live behind Traefik
actions.summary: Check a single public host's end-to-end readiness through Traefik — the cutover preflight read.
actions.description: Check a single public host's end-to-end readiness through Traefik — the cutover preflight read. Joins /api/http/routers and /api/http/services into ONE compact verdict: is there an enabled router matching Host(`<host>`), a healthy service behind it, and at least one UP backend? Returns "host", "ready" (true only when nothing is wrong), the matched router and resolved service (name/provider/status/errors), backend counts (up/down/total) with the DOWN backend URLs, and a "failures" list of operator-readable reasons: missing_router, router_not_enabled, router_errors, missing_service, service_not_enabled, service_errors, no_up_backends, backend_down. Use this before a DNS/origin cutover instead of the raw 4 MiB inventory dumps.
side_effects: Two read-only HTTP GETs to the Traefik API.

args.name: host
args.description: The public host to check, as it appears in the router's Host(`…`) rule — a bare DNS name, no scheme/port/path (e.g. app.va1.example.com).

examples.title: Preflight a host before an origin cutover

actions.title: GET /api/http/middlewares
actions.summary: List every HTTP middleware (auth, rate-limit, headers, redirects, retries, circuit-breaker, etc.) with its config, status, and error[]. Use to confirm a middleware is configured as expected and not in error.
actions.description: List every HTTP middleware (auth, rate-limit, headers, redirects, retries, circuit-breaker, etc.) with its config, status, and error[]. Use to confirm a middleware is configured as expected and not in error.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /api/http/routers
actions.summary: List every HTTP router with its rule, entrypoints, service, middlewares, TLS config, and — critically — its status ("enabled" | "warning" | "disabled") and error[] list. To find broken routing, look for any router whose status is not "enabled" and read its error[]. Names are "<name>@<provider>" (e.g. my-router@docker).
actions.description: List every HTTP router with its rule, entrypoints, service, middlewares, TLS config, and — critically — its status ("enabled" | "warning" | "disabled") and error[] list. To find broken routing, look for any router whose status is not "enabled" and read its error[]. Names are "<name>@<provider>" (e.g. my-router@docker).
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /api/http/services
actions.summary: List every HTTP service with its load-balancer config, status, error[], and serverStatus — a per-backend map of URL to "UP"/"DOWN". This is the "which upstream is down?" read: a service with a DOWN server (or a non-"enabled" status) is failing health checks.
actions.description: List every HTTP service with its load-balancer config, status, error[], and serverStatus — a per-backend map of URL to "UP"/"DOWN". This is the "which upstream is down?" read: a service with a DOWN server (or a non-"enabled" status) is failing health checks.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: List HTTP services, compact health summary
actions.summary: List every HTTP service as a compact, name-sorted health summary — the preflight-sized view of /api/http/services without its multi-megabyte load-balancer config.
actions.description: List every HTTP service as a compact, name-sorted health summary — the preflight-sized view of /api/http/services without its multi-megabyte load-balancer config. One row per service: name, provider, status, error_count + errors, backend counts (up/down/total), and the URLs of only the DOWN backends. Set only_unhealthy=true to return just the services that need attention (not "enabled", carrying errors, a DOWN backend, or a load-balancer with no UP backend). Use traefik.http_services for the full raw load-balancer config of a single service.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

args.name: only_unhealthy
args.description: When true, return only services that need attention — status not "enabled", with errors, with a DOWN backend, or a load-balancer with no UP backend. Default false returns every service.

examples.title: Compact health summary of every HTTP service
Only the services that need attention

actions.title: Recent 4xx responses from access log
actions.summary: Greps the Traefik access log for 4xx responses and tails the most recent. Matches both the JSON format (DownstreamStatus field) and the default CLF format (status code after the request line). Read-only.
actions.description: Greps the Traefik access log for 4xx responses and tails the most recent. Matches both the JSON format (DownstreamStatus field) and the default CLF format (status code after the request line). Read-only.

args.name: lines
args.description: How many recent 4xx lines to return.

actions.title: Recent 5xx responses from access log
actions.summary: Greps the Traefik access log for 5xx responses and tails the most recent — the front-door view of backend failures. Matches both the JSON format (DownstreamStatus field) and the default CLF format. Read-only.
actions.description: Greps the Traefik access log for 5xx responses and tails the most recent — the front-door view of backend failures. Matches both the JSON format (DownstreamStatus field) and the default CLF format. Read-only.

args.name: lines
args.description: How many recent 5xx lines to return.

actions.title: GET /metrics
actions.summary: Show Prometheus metrics in text exposition format (requires --metrics.prometheus=true): per-entrypoint/router/service request counts, durations, open connections, and TLS cert expiry gauges. Served on the traefik entrypoint (:8080) by default unless metrics.prometheus.entryPoint moves it.
actions.description: Show Prometheus metrics in text exposition format (requires --metrics.prometheus=true): per-entrypoint/router/service request counts, durations, open connections, and TLS cert expiry gauges. Served on the traefik entrypoint (:8080) by default unless metrics.prometheus.entryPoint moves it.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik metrics endpoint.

actions.title: GET /api/overview
actions.summary: Show dashboard summary — counts of HTTP/TCP/UDP routers, services, and middlewares by state (enabled/warning/errored), enabled providers, and which features (metrics, tracing) are on. The fastest "is anything broken?" read before drilling into the per-router inventory.
actions.description: Show dashboard summary — counts of HTTP/TCP/UDP routers, services, and middlewares by state (enabled/warning/errored), enabled providers, and which features (metrics, tracing) are on. The fastest "is anything broken?" read before drilling into the per-router inventory.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /ping
actions.summary: Check liveness. Returns 200 with body "OK" when healthy, or the configured terminating status (default 503) during graceful shutdown. Requires --ping=true; served on the ping entrypoint (the traefik entrypoint / :8080 by default, unless moved to a dedicated one — point TRAEFIK_URL at it then).
actions.description: Check liveness. Returns 200 with body "OK" when healthy, or the configured terminating status (default 503) during graceful shutdown. Requires --ping=true; served on the ping entrypoint (the traefik entrypoint / :8080 by default, unless moved to a dedicated one — point TRAEFIK_URL at it then).
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik ping endpoint.

actions.title: GET /api/rawdata
actions.summary: Show the complete dynamic configuration as Traefik currently sees it — all routers, services, and middlewares across HTTP/TCP/UDP, each with its errors, plus the "usedBy" dependency graph tying routers to services and middlewares. The single most complete read for "what is the actual live config and what depends on what?". Larger than the per-kind endpoints.
actions.description: Show the complete dynamic configuration as Traefik currently sees it — all routers, services, and middlewares across HTTP/TCP/UDP, each with its errors, plus the "usedBy" dependency graph tying routers to services and middlewares. The single most complete read for "what is the actual live config and what depends on what?". Larger than the per-kind endpoints.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

examples.title: Full live dynamic config + dependency graph

actions.title: GET /api/tcp/routers
actions.summary: List every TCP router with its rule (HostSNI/...), entrypoints, service, TLS passthrough config, status, and error[]. Use for TCP/SNI routing (databases, message brokers, raw TLS) the same way http_routers covers HTTP.
actions.description: List every TCP router with its rule (HostSNI/...), entrypoints, service, TLS passthrough config, status, and error[]. Use for TCP/SNI routing (databases, message brokers, raw TLS) the same way http_routers covers HTTP.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /api/tcp/services
actions.summary: List every TCP service with its load-balancer/weighted config, status, and error[]. The TCP counterpart to http_services.
actions.description: List every TCP service with its load-balancer/weighted config, status, and error[]. The TCP counterpart to http_services.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /api/udp/routers
actions.summary: List every UDP router (and its service binding) with status and error[]. Use for UDP entrypoints (DNS, QUIC, syslog) routing visibility. Pair with /api/udp/services via rawdata if you need the service side.
actions.description: List every UDP router (and its service binding) with status and error[]. Use for UDP entrypoints (DNS, QUIC, syslog) routing visibility. Pair with /api/udp/services via rawdata if you need the service side.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

actions.title: GET /api/version
actions.summary: Show Traefik version, codename, and build/start date. Use to confirm which release is running (v2 vs v3 behaviour) and that the API answers.
actions.description: Show Traefik version, codename, and build/start date. Use to confirm which release is running (v2 vs v3 behaviour) and that the API answers.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Traefik API.

packs.name: Typesense search
packs.description: Read-only diagnostics for a Typesense search node over its HTTP API: health and node/raft state, per-endpoint request stats and system metrics, the collection catalog and individual schemas, API-key metadata, and a tail of slow requests from the server log. One admin API key, streamed over curl stdin, unlocks the stats/metrics/debug endpoints a search-only key cannot read.

actions.title: GET /collections/{name}
actions.summary: Returns the schema and document count for a single collection by name. Use when you already know the collection and want just its definition. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Returns the schema and document count for a single collection by name. Use when you already know the collection and want just its definition. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /collections/{name} endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

actions.title: GET /collections
actions.summary: Lists all collections on the node with their full schemas and document counts. Use to inventory what is indexed and how big each collection is. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Lists all collections on the node with their full schemas and document counts. Use to inventory what is indexed and how big each collection is. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /collections endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

actions.title: GET /debug
actions.summary: Show node version and raft state. The "state" field reports this node's role in the cluster: 1 = LEADER, 4 = FOLLOWER. This is how you read which node is leader vs follower per node. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Show node version and raft state. The "state" field reports this node's role in the cluster: 1 = LEADER, 4 = FOLLOWER. This is how you read which node is leader vs follower per node. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /debug endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

actions.title: GET /health
actions.summary: Check liveness for the Typesense node. Returns {"ok": true} when healthy, and surfaces resource-exhaustion states (out of memory, out of disk) when the node has stopped accepting writes. No API key required.
actions.description: Check liveness for the Typesense node. Returns {"ok": true} when healthy, and surfaces resource-exhaustion states (out of memory, out of disk) when the node has stopped accepting writes. No API key required.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /health endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

actions.title: GET /keys
actions.summary: Lists API key metadata — the key prefix, description, scoped actions, and collections each key may access. The secret value is never returned; only which keys exist and what they are allowed to do. Sensitive: it discloses the set of credentials configured on the node. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Lists API key metadata — the key prefix, description, scoped actions, and collections each key may access. The secret value is never returned; only which keys exist and what they are allowed to do. Sensitive: it discloses the set of credentials configured on the node. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /keys endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.
Returns key metadata only (prefixes and scopes); secrets are not exposed.

actions.title: GET /metrics.json
actions.summary: Show system and process metrics for the node — CPU utilization, memory usage, and disk usage. Use to see whether the node is resource-constrained. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Show system and process metrics for the node — CPU utilization, memory usage, and disk usage. Use to see whether the node is resource-constrained. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /metrics.json endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

actions.title: Recent slow requests from the server log
actions.summary: Tail slow requests from the Typesense server log — slow requests are written to the server log prefixed "SLOW REQUEST" (enable with --log-slow-requests-time-ms). This greps the log for those lines and tails the most recent ones. Read-only.
actions.description: Tail slow requests from the Typesense server log — slow requests are written to the server log prefixed "SLOW REQUEST" (enable with --log-slow-requests-time-ms). This greps the log for those lines and tails the most recent ones. Read-only.
side_effects: Reads the Typesense server log file.

actions.title: GET /stats.json
actions.summary: Show per-endpoint request statistics over the last 10 seconds — requests-per-second counters and latency_ms breakdowns per API path. Use to see which endpoints are hot and how slow they are right now. Requires the admin API key.
actions.description: Show per-endpoint request statistics over the last 10 seconds — requests-per-second counters and latency_ms breakdowns per API path. Use to see which endpoints are hot and how slow they are right now. Requires the admin API key.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the Typesense /stats.json endpoint.
Read-only — never writes or mutates data.

packs.name: HashiCorp Vault operations
packs.description: Vault status, seal state, auth/audit/secret backends, mount listing, token & lease introspection, plus operator surface for incident response: revoke lease, revoke leases by prefix, operator step-down, emergency seal. Auth via VAULT_ADDR + VAULT_TOKEN on the runner host. Does NOT include unseal — that requires quorum and shouldn't be automated through a runner.

actions.title: vault list cubbyhole
actions.summary: Lists the runner token's own cubbyhole (each token sees only its own).
actions.description: Lists the runner token's own cubbyhole (each token sees only its own).
side_effects: Read-only — values not returned, only keys.

actions.title: vault lease revoke -prefix <prefix>
actions.summary: Revoke every lease under a path prefix. Use after a compromise to invalidate every dynamic credential for one engine. Irreversible for issued credentials — affected services must re-authenticate.
actions.description: Revoke every lease under a path prefix. Use after a compromise to invalidate every dynamic credential for one engine. Irreversible for issued credentials — affected services must re-authenticate.
side_effects: Every lease under the prefix revoked.
Dynamic credentials (DB users, AWS keys, etc.) revoked at the upstream.
Affected clients see auth failures until they request new credentials.

actions.title: vault list sys/leases/lookup/<prefix>
actions.summary: Count of active leases under one mount prefix.
actions.description: Count of active leases under one mount prefix.

actions.title: vault audit list
actions.summary: List enabled audit devices.
actions.description: List enabled audit devices.

actions.title: vault auth list
actions.summary: List all auth method mounts.
actions.description: List all auth method mounts.

actions.title: vault secrets list
actions.summary: List all secret engine mount points.
actions.description: List all secret engine mount points.

actions.title: vault policy list
actions.summary: List all policy names.
actions.description: List all policy names.

actions.title: vault operator seal
actions.summary: Seal Vault. ALL clients lose access to secrets immediately; ongoing requests fail. Recovery requires unseal keys from a quorum of shareholders. Use only for compromised-cluster containment.
actions.description: Seal Vault. ALL clients lose access to secrets immediately; ongoing requests fail. Recovery requires unseal keys from a quorum of shareholders. Use only for compromised-cluster containment.
side_effects: Vault sealed; all secret operations fail.
Active leases continue to be enforced by downstreams but cannot be renewed.
Recovery needs unseal keys (auto-unseal still requires KMS/cloud HSM).

actions.title: vault operator step-down
actions.summary: Forces the active node to step down. A standby will take over within seconds. Use this to force a failover or to drain a node before maintenance.
actions.description: Forces the active node to step down. A standby will take over within seconds. Use this to force a failover or to drain a node before maintenance.
side_effects: Brief unavailability during leader election (~1-5 seconds).
In-flight requests on the stepping-down node fail.

actions.title: vault policy read <name>
actions.summary: Read the HCL document for one policy.
actions.description: Read the HCL document for one policy.

actions.title: vault lease revoke <id>
actions.summary: Revokes one lease — the credential becomes invalid immediately. Anyone using it gets a 403 from the downstream system.
actions.description: Revokes one lease — the credential becomes invalid immediately. Anyone using it gets a 403 from the downstream system.
side_effects: Lease is revoked; underlying credential disabled.
In-flight requests using the credential will fail.

actions.title: vault operator seal-status
actions.summary: Show sealed/unsealed state + threshold + progress.
actions.description: Show sealed/unsealed state + threshold + progress.

actions.title: vault status
actions.summary: Show top-level Vault status: sealed, version, HA mode, active node.
actions.description: Show top-level Vault status: sealed, version, HA mode, active node.

actions.title: vault token lookup
actions.summary: Show metadata on the current token (the runner's) — accessor, policies, ttl. Useful to debug policy denials. The token itself (data.id) is redacted from the output.
actions.description: Show metadata on the current token (the runner's) — accessor, policies, ttl. Useful to debug policy denials. The token itself (data.id) is redacted from the output.
side_effects: Read-only; the token value (data.id) returned by the API is redacted before output.

packs.name: Vector observability pipeline
packs.description: Read-only ops for a Vector (vector.dev) pipeline running on the runner host: version + compiled-component inventory, offline config validation, the configured topology as Graphviz DOT, a bounded live event tap, plus health and per-component throughput reads over the local API. CLI subcommands talk to the local binary; the API reads hit 127.0.0.1:8686.

actions.title: POST /graphql (component metrics)
actions.summary: Show per-component throughput from the Vector GraphQL API: received and sent event totals for every source, transform, and sink, plus sent-bytes for sinks. GraphQL API — Vector <= 0.54 ONLY. On Vector >= 0.55 (gRPC migration, April 2026) the /graphql endpoint was REMOVED and this returns 404; run vector.version first and, if >= 0.55, use gRPC tooling instead. Requires api.enabled=true; binds 127.0.0.1:8686.
actions.description: Show per-component throughput from the Vector GraphQL API: received and sent event totals for every source, transform, and sink, plus sent-bytes for sinks. GraphQL API — Vector <= 0.54 ONLY. On Vector >= 0.55 (gRPC migration, April 2026) the /graphql endpoint was REMOVED and this returns 404; run vector.version first and, if >= 0.55, use gRPC tooling instead. Requires api.enabled=true; binds 127.0.0.1:8686.
side_effects: One read-only GraphQL POST (a query, not a mutation) to the local Vector API.

argv: curl -sS -X POST "${VECTOR_API:-http://127.0.0.1:8686}/graphql" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data '{"query":"query { sources { edges { node { componentId metrics { receivedEventsTotal { receivedEventsTotal } sentEventsTotal { sentEventsTotal } } } } } transforms { edges { node { componentId metrics { receivedEventsTotal { receivedEventsTotal } sentEventsTotal { sentEventsTotal } } } } } sinks { edges { node { componentId metrics { receivedEventsTotal { receivedEventsTotal } sentEventsTotal { sentEventsTotal } sentBytesTotal { sentBytesTotal } } } } } }"}'

actions.title: vector graph
actions.summary: Render the configured Vector topology as a Graphviz DOT graph — the source -> transform -> sink wiring read from the config file. Offline: reads the config, emits DOT, connects to nothing. Pipe the output to `dot` to draw it. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
actions.description: Render the configured Vector topology as a Graphviz DOT graph — the source -> transform -> sink wiring read from the config file. Offline: reads the config, emits DOT, connects to nothing. Pipe the output to `dot` to draw it. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
side_effects: Reads the Vector config and emits its topology as DOT.

actions.title: GET /health
actions.summary: Check liveness of the local Vector API. Returns {"ok":true} (HTTP 200) when serving, or {"ok":false} (HTTP 503) while draining/shutting down. Works on ANY Vector version — this endpoint survived the v0.55 gRPC migration. Requires api.enabled=true in the Vector config; binds 127.0.0.1:8686.
actions.description: Check liveness of the local Vector API. Returns {"ok":true} (HTTP 200) when serving, or {"ok":false} (HTTP 503) while draining/shutting down. Works on ANY Vector version — this endpoint survived the v0.55 gRPC migration. Requires api.enabled=true in the Vector config; binds 127.0.0.1:8686.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the local Vector API.

actions.title: vector list --format json
actions.summary: List the sources, transforms, and sinks compiled into this Vector binary, as JSON. This is the static component catalog of the build — not the running topology (use vector.graph for what is actually configured). Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
actions.description: List the sources, transforms, and sinks compiled into this Vector binary, as JSON. This is the static component catalog of the build — not the running topology (use vector.graph for what is actually configured). Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
side_effects: Reads the local Vector binary's component list.

actions.title: vector tap --outputs-of
actions.summary: Sample live events flowing OUT of a named component for a bounded window, as JSON, then auto-exit. The run terminates after duration_ms — NOT on --limit alone, which caps events PER sampling interval, not total, so without a duration a tap can run indefinitely. Adds slight overhead on the running Vector instance while sampling. Connects to the local API (tap is API-backed); component is matched against configured component IDs and supports glob patterns.
actions.description: Sample live events flowing OUT of a named component for a bounded window, as JSON, then auto-exit. The run terminates after duration_ms — NOT on --limit alone, which caps events PER sampling interval, not total, so without a duration a tap can run indefinitely. Adds slight overhead on the running Vector instance while sampling. Connects to the local API (tap is API-backed); component is matched against configured component IDs and supports glob patterns.
side_effects: Subscribes to a component's output stream on the local Vector instance.
Slight runtime overhead while sampling; auto-exits after duration_ms.
Read-only — observes events, never modifies the pipeline.

args.name: component
args.description: Component ID (or glob) whose outputs to tap, e.g. parse_json or "transform_*".

args.name: duration_ms
args.description: How long to sample before auto-exiting, in milliseconds. This is what bounds the run.

args.name: limit
args.description: Max events per sampling interval (per-interval cap, NOT a total — duration_ms bounds the run).

examples.title: Sample a sink's input briefly with a small per-interval cap

actions.title: vector validate --no-environment
actions.summary: Validate a Vector config file offline — checks syntax and topology. Runs with --no-environment, so it checks structure only and skips all network sink healthchecks and environment probing — it never connects to anything. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
actions.description: Validate a Vector config file offline — checks syntax and topology. Runs with --no-environment, so it checks structure only and skips all network sink healthchecks and environment probing — it never connects to anything. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
side_effects: Reads and parses a Vector config file on the runner host.
Read-only — no network healthchecks (--no-environment), no reload.

args.name: config_path
args.description: Path to the Vector config file to validate.

actions.title: vector --version
actions.summary: Print the Vector binary version and build metadata. Run this FIRST: it tells you whether the observability API is GraphQL (Vector <= 0.54) or gRPC (>= 0.55, April 2026). That determines whether the GraphQL component_metrics action will work or 404. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.
actions.description: Print the Vector binary version and build metadata. Run this FIRST: it tells you whether the observability API is GraphQL (Vector <= 0.54) or gRPC (>= 0.55, April 2026). That determines whether the GraphQL component_metrics action will work or 404. Talks only to the local binary — needs no API.

examples.title: Show version (and pick GraphQL vs gRPC)

packs.name: VictoriaLogs queries
packs.description: Read-only LogsQL access to VictoriaLogs over its HTTP API: search log entries, hit histograms over time, stats aggregations (instant + range), and field / stream discovery. Express time ranges inside the LogsQL query via the _time filter. One base URL serves single-node and vmauth-fronted deployments; multitenancy via optional headers.

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/field_names
actions.summary: List the log field names matching a query, each with a hit count. Use to discover what fields exist before querying. Doubles as the pack's connectivity check.
actions.description: List the log field names matching a query, each with a hit count. Use to discover what fields exist before querying. Doubles as the pack's connectivity check.

args.name: query
args.description: LogsQL scope, e.g. _time:1h app:api. Defaults to all logs.

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/field_values
actions.summary: List the most frequent values of one log field, each with a hit count. Use to enumerate levels, namespaces, hosts, or any other dimension.
actions.description: List the most frequent values of one log field, each with a hit count. Use to enumerate levels, namespaces, hosts, or any other dimension.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the VictoriaLogs query API.

args.name: field
args.description: Field name to enumerate values for, e.g. level, app, _stream.

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of values to return.

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/hits
actions.summary: Count log entries matching a LogsQL query, bucketed over time by step — the histogram behind "how many errors per hour?". Bound the range with a _time filter in the query.
actions.description: Count log entries matching a LogsQL query, bucketed over time by step — the histogram behind "how many errors per hour?". Bound the range with a _time filter in the query.

examples.title: Errors per hour over the last day

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/query
actions.summary: Run a LogsQL query and return matching log entries as newline-delimited JSON. Bound the result with a _time filter in the query (e.g. "_time:5m error") and/or sort + limit — without a _time filter this scans all stored logs. The limit arg caps how many entries come back.
actions.description: Run a LogsQL query and return matching log entries as newline-delimited JSON. Bound the result with a _time filter in the query (e.g. "_time:5m error") and/or sort + limit — without a _time filter this scans all stored logs. The limit arg caps how many entries come back.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the VictoriaLogs query API.
Read-only — never ingests or deletes logs.

args.name: query
args.description: LogsQL query, e.g. _time:5m error, or {app="api"} | stats by (level) count().

args.name: limit
args.description: Maximum number of log entries to return.

examples.title: Errors for one app, newest first

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/stats_query
actions.summary: Show instant stats over logs — a LogsQL query containing a "| stats" pipe, evaluated at one point in time and returned in Prometheus instant-query format. Use for totals like counts or sums grouped by a field.
actions.description: Show instant stats over logs — a LogsQL query containing a "| stats" pipe, evaluated at one point in time and returned in Prometheus instant-query format. Use for totals like counts or sums grouped by a field.

args.name: query

examples.title: Log count by level in the last hour

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/stats_query_range
actions.summary: Show stats over logs across a time range — a LogsQL query containing a "| stats" pipe, evaluated at each step and returned in Prometheus range-query (matrix) format. Use to chart an aggregate over time. Bound the range with a _time filter in the query.
actions.description: Show stats over logs across a time range — a LogsQL query containing a "| stats" pipe, evaluated at each step and returned in Prometheus range-query (matrix) format. Use to chart an aggregate over time. Bound the range with a _time filter in the query.
side_effects: One read-only HTTP GET to the VictoriaLogs query API.

args.name: query
args.description: LogsQL with a | stats pipe, e.g. _time:6h * | stats count() logs.

actions.title: GET /select/logsql/streams
actions.summary: List the log streams matching a query, each with a hit count. A stream is the set of logs sharing the same stream labels (app, host, container, …). Use to see which sources are sending logs.
actions.description: List the log streams matching a query, each with a hit count. A stream is the set of logs sharing the same stream labels (app, host, container, …). Use to see which sources are sending logs.

args.name: query

packs.name: VictoriaMetrics queries
packs.description: Read-only PromQL / MetricsQL access to VictoriaMetrics over its HTTP API: instant and range queries, series and label discovery, and the VM-specific status endpoints (TSDB cardinality, active queries, top queries). One base URL serves single-node, cluster (vmselect), and vmauth-fronted deployments.

actions.title: GET /api/v1/status/active_queries
actions.summary: List the queries VictoriaMetrics is executing right now — their text, how long they have been running, and the client address. Use to catch a runaway or slow query in the act.
actions.description: List the queries VictoriaMetrics is executing right now — their text, how long they have been running, and the client address. Use to catch a runaway or slow query in the act.

actions.title: GET /api/v1/labels

packs.name: WireGuard VPN
packs.description: WireGuard state — interfaces, peers, transfer counts, last handshakes — plus operator actions: bring iface up/down via wg-quick, remove a peer from a live iface. Use to debug connectivity or evict a compromised peer.

actions.title: wg-quick down <iface>
actions.summary: Tear down a WireGuard interface. All active sessions through it drop. PostDown rules run (typically cleaning up routes/firewall).
actions.description: Tear down a WireGuard interface. All active sessions through it drop. PostDown rules run (typically cleaning up routes/firewall).
side_effects: Routing + firewall rules cleaned up by PostDown.

actions.title: wg-quick up <iface>
actions.summary: Bring up a WireGuard interface defined in /etc/wireguard/<iface>.conf. Applies the address, peers, and PostUp rules.
actions.description: Bring up a WireGuard interface defined in /etc/wireguard/<iface>.conf. Applies the address, peers, and PostUp rules.
side_effects: Interface comes up with the configured peers.

actions.title: wg set <iface> peer <pubkey> remove
actions.summary: Remove one peer from a running WireGuard interface. Its tunnel sessions drop. The peer can no longer connect until added back (or the config file is reloaded).
actions.description: Remove one peer from a running WireGuard interface. Its tunnel sessions drop. The peer can no longer connect until added back (or the config file is reloaded).
side_effects: Change is runtime-only; iface config file unchanged.

actions.title: wg show
actions.summary: List all interfaces with peers + endpoints + last-handshake.
actions.description: List all interfaces with peers + endpoints + last-handshake.

actions.title: wg show all endpoints
actions.summary: Show per-peer remote endpoint (last known).
actions.description: Show per-peer remote endpoint (last known).

actions.title: wg show all latest-handshakes
actions.summary: Show per-peer epoch of last handshake. 0 means never seen.
actions.description: Show per-peer epoch of last handshake. 0 means never seen.

actions.title: wg show all transfer
actions.summary: Show per-peer cumulative bytes rx/tx. Use to confirm "is the tunnel actually carrying traffic?".
actions.description: Show per-peer cumulative bytes rx/tx. Use to confirm "is the tunnel actually carrying traffic?".

actions.title: wg showconf <iface>
actions.summary: Show the effective config for one WireGuard interface (`[Interface]`/`[Peer]` sections, peer pubkeys, allowed-ips, endpoints). PrivateKey and PresharedKey lines are masked at the source.
actions.description: Show the effective config for one WireGuard interface (`[Interface]`/`[Peer]` sections, peer pubkeys, allowed-ips, endpoints). PrivateKey and PresharedKey lines are masked at the source.
side_effects: `wg showconf` emits the interface PrivateKey and per-peer PresharedKey; the action masks both at the source before any output leaves the host.

packs.name: ZFS pool + dataset operations
packs.description: Pool status, dataset inventory, snapshot listing, scrub status, ARC stats, plus operator surface for storage incident response: scrub start/stop, clear pool errors, take/destroy snapshots, dataset rollback.

actions.title: /proc/spl/kstat/zfs/arcstats
actions.summary: Show ARC cache statistics — size, hit ratio, eviction counts.
actions.description: Show ARC cache statistics — size, hit ratio, eviction counts.

actions.title: zpool clear <pool> [device]
actions.summary: Clear pool error counters and re-online any FAULTED device that has come back. Use after replacing a failed disk, or after a transient I/O storm cleared. Does NOT touch on-disk data.
actions.description: Clear pool error counters and re-online any FAULTED device that has come back. Use after replacing a failed disk, or after a transient I/O storm cleared. Does NOT touch on-disk data.
side_effects: Read, write, checksum error counters reset.
FAULTED devices marked ONLINE if reachable.

actions.title: zfs list
actions.summary: List all datasets with used + avail + refer + mountpoint.
actions.description: List all datasets with used + avail + refer + mountpoint.

actions.title: zfs rollback <dataset>@<snap>
actions.summary: Roll a dataset back to an earlier snapshot. ALL changes after the snapshot are PERMANENTLY discarded. Intermediate snapshots between current and target are also destroyed (require -r flag). Use only after confirming current data is salvageable from elsewhere or the loss is acceptable.
actions.description: Roll a dataset back to an earlier snapshot. ALL changes after the snapshot are PERMANENTLY discarded. Intermediate snapshots between current and target are also destroyed (require -r flag). Use only after confirming current data is salvageable from elsewhere or the loss is acceptable.
side_effects: Dataset reverts to the snapshot's state.
All data written after the snapshot is destroyed.
Running processes with open file handles may break.

argv: if [ '{{ args.force }}' = 'true' ]; then zfs rollback -r '{{ args.dataset }}@{{ args.snap }}'; else zfs rollback '{{ args.dataset }}@{{ args.snap }}'; fi

actions.title: zpool iostat (5s sample)
actions.summary: Show one 5-second iostat sample per pool.
actions.description: Show one 5-second iostat sample per pool.

actions.title: zpool list
actions.summary: List all pools with size + alloc + free + fragmentation.
actions.description: List all pools with size + alloc + free + fragmentation.

actions.title: zpool status -v
actions.summary: Show per-pool vdev tree, scrub state, errors. Use to debug DEGRADED pools.
actions.description: Show per-pool vdev tree, scrub state, errors. Use to debug DEGRADED pools.

argv: if [ -z "{{ args.pool }}" ]; then zpool status -v; else zpool status -v "{{ args.pool }}"; fi

actions.title: zfs get all <dataset>
actions.summary: List all properties of one dataset (compression, dedup, atime, quota, etc).
actions.description: List all properties of one dataset (compression, dedup, atime, quota, etc).

actions.title: zpool scrub -s <pool>
actions.summary: Stop an in-progress scrub. Use when scrub is impacting workload and the verification can wait for a maintenance window.
actions.description: Stop an in-progress scrub. Use when scrub is impacting workload and the verification can wait for a maintenance window.
side_effects: Progress lost; next scrub starts from zero.
Any checksum errors found so far remain in the counters.

actions.title: zfs destroy <pool>/<dataset>@<snap>
actions.summary: Delete one ZFS snapshot. Reclaims the snapshot-exclusive blocks immediately. Irreversible. Confirm the snapshot is not the only thing protecting against a recent ransomware/oops event before destroying.
actions.description: Delete one ZFS snapshot. Reclaims the snapshot-exclusive blocks immediately. Irreversible. Confirm the snapshot is not the only thing protecting against a recent ransomware/oops event before destroying.
side_effects: Blocks unique to the snapshot freed.
Restore from this snapshot no longer possible.

actions.title: zfs list -t snapshot
actions.summary: List all snapshots with used + refer + creation.
actions.description: List all snapshots with used + refer + creation.

actions.title: zpool scrub <pool>
actions.summary: Starts a scrub on one pool. Re-reads every block and verifies checksums.
actions.description: Starts a scrub on one pool. Re-reads every block and verifies checksums.
side_effects: Heavy disk read load for hours-to-days.
Pool stays online; IO latency increases.

actions.title: zfs snapshot <dataset>@<name>
actions.summary: Takes an atomic snapshot of one dataset. Copy-on-write, instant.
actions.description: Takes an atomic snapshot of one dataset. Copy-on-write, instant.
side_effects: Snapshot is created; consumes increasing space as live dataset diverges.

packs.description: Cluster + 4lw command + watch + session introspection. Read-only. Set ZK_SERVERS env var (host:port,host:port,…). All four-letter words need to be in zoo.cfg's `4lw.commands.whitelist`.

actions.summary: Show the server's effective configuration.
actions.description: Show the server's effective configuration.

actions.title: cons (all connections)
actions.summary: List all client connections + their session + per-session stats.
actions.description: List all client connections + their session + per-session stats.

actions.title: envi (server env)
actions.summary: Show the server's JVM + OS environment via the `envi` four-letter word.
actions.description: Show the server's JVM + OS environment via the `envi` four-letter word. This deliberately dumps the node's environment — JVM system properties and OS/user details (java.class.path, java.io.tmpdir, java.home, user.name, user.home, user.dir, os.*) — which discloses install paths, usernames, and topology, and on some builds carries values injected via system properties. The runner's redaction is a fail-closed backstop, not a guarantee — it is pattern-bound and can miss a bespoke secret whose name and value match no rule.
side_effects: Read-only, but exposes the server's full JVM/OS environment (may include secrets).

packs.name: zot OCI registry
packs.description: Read-only inspection of a zot OCI registry over its HTTP API: the OCI distribution endpoints (version check, catalog, tags, manifests) plus the zot extensions — registry config/health (mgmt), Prometheus metrics, and a GraphQL search query that returns per-repo size and a newest-image vulnerability summary. Optional basic-auth credentials are streamed over curl stdin; many zot deployments allow anonymous read.

actions.description: Show Prometheus-format metrics for the registry via the zot metrics extension — request counts, storage, and runtime gauges. Requires the full (extended) build with the metrics extension enabled in config, and typically auth (set ZOT_BASICAUTH). Read-only.

The mgmt extension is on by default in the full build but requires auth and is denied to anonymous clients, so set ZOT_BASICAUTH. Read-only.

The mgmt extension is on by default in the full build but requires auth and is denied to anonymous clients, so set ZOT_BASICAUTH. Read-only.

This is how you get per-repo storage size + CVE summary — zot has no plain storage-usage endpoint, and garbage collection is not inspectable over HTTP. The GraphQL query is read-only (a RepoListWithNewestImage read). Read-only.
